name: 'I', 'Y', 'T's
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and your arms straight by your side

2. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

3. Let your arms hang by your sides with your hands pointing at the floor

4. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down.

5. Begin the movement by raising both arms directly in front in a controlled manner, ensuring they stay straight

6. Pause and then lower the arms under control to the start position, then repeat but this time raise the arms at a 45-degree angle so you form a 'Y' with them

7. Pause and then lower the arms under control to the start position, then repeat but this time raise the arms straight out to the side so you form a T' with the body

8. Return to the start position and then repeat

9. As you raise the arms it's important that you don't shrug your shoulders up to your ears and that you keep your shoulder blades retracted. Also, avoid any momentum with your arms, ensuring you keep a neutral spine throughout

name: 3 Part Shoulder Warm-Up
description:
1. Grab a light resistance band with both hands

2. You should have a neutral spine with your head facing forward and your chest up

3. Keep your core engaged and shoulders depressed down for all 3 movements

4. First movement is 'band over and backs'

5. Second is 'band pull aparts'. Perform with the band below shoulder height if you struggle to keep your shoulders down

6. Third is 'band face pulls'. Perform with the band anchored above shoulder height if you struggle to keep your shoulders down

name: Ab Roller
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine

2. Grab the handles of the ab roller with both hands and position it on the floor underneath your chest. Your arms should be straight but not locked out

3. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then simultaneously push the roller and extend your hips forward in a slow controlled manner

5. As you move forward your torso will lower towards the floor but ensure your hips stay level. Your hips shouldn’t drop towards the floor and be careful not to extend your spine

6. Keep your head neutral and looking at the floor, making sure to keep a neutral spine and constant tension in your core

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause slightly and then return to the start position by pulling the roller back towards you. Your torso should raise up as you pull in towards yourself

8. Be careful to never pull the roller underneath yourself

name: Active Leg Lowering To Bolster.
description:
1. Lie on your back with the back of your legs on a foam roller, just above the ankle

2. Place your arms flat by your side

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise your two legs (keeping them relaxed) as high as possible without the knee bending

4. Start the movement by engaging your core and lowering one of your legs until you touch the foam roller, maintaining the position with the other leg

5. Pause slightly and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core

6. Keep a neutral spine throughout

name: Against Wall Split Stance Calf Stretch
Alternate March
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor, heels on the floor, and both legs at 90-degrees. Place your arms straight by your side or on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lift one leg so your hip is at a 90-degree angle. As you raise your legs it’s important that you keep your back flat to the floor

4. When you reach the top, pause and then lower your leg to the start position in a slow controlled manner. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower, keeping constant tension in your core

5. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternate March - Hips 90 Degrees
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor, heels on the floor, and both legs at 90-degrees. Place your arms straight by your side or on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lift one leg so your hip is at a 90-degree angle. As you raise your legs it’s important that you keep your back flat to the floor

4. When you reach the top, pause and then lift the other leg

5. Pause again and then lower your legs one after the other in a slow controlled manner. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower, keeping constant tension in your core

6. Reverse the order for the next rep

name: Alternating Barbell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Position a barbell so its chest height in a rack or have a partner carefully lift the barbell on your back

2. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

3. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

4. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

5. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

6. Keep on the toes for the back foot

7. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

8. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

9. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

10. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

11. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating BW Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine. Hands can be by your side or out in front for balance

3. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

4. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

5. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

6. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

7. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Dumbbell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

10. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Dumbbell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Dumbbell Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you dumbbell in each hand and have arms by your side

3. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Dumbbell Zottman Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbell in, so your palm is facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep, forearm, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Flat Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set up on a flat bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering one of the dumbbells until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

6) Repeat with the other side.

name: Alternating Floor Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Take up a seated position on the floor and pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering one of the dumbbells with the arm are at a 45-degree angle to the body until your elbow touches the floor.

4) Control the dumbbell down and be careful not to bang your elbow off the floor.

5) While keeping your back flat to the floor, proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Alternating Forward Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

3. Keep on the toes with the back foot

4. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

5. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

6. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

7. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Incline Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. Begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in its starting position

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Incline Dumbbell Neutral Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

5. Begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in its starting position

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Incline Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set a bench up to an incline, typically between 15-30 degrees and then carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering one of the dumbbells until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

6) Repeat with the other side.

name: Alternating Incline Dumbbell Supinating Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

5. Begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

6. As you bring it up, externally rotate the dumbbell so you finish with your palm facing up

7. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in its starting position

8. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

9. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner by reversing the movement, ensuring constant tension on your bicep

10. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Incline Zottman Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. Begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in its starting position

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbell in, so your palm is facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep, forearm, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Kettlebell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

10. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Kettlebell Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you kettlebell in each hand and have arms by your side

3. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Leg Lowering - Hover To Tap
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs off the floor at 90-degrees. Place your arms straight by your side or on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drop one foot towards the floor in a slow controlled manner. Stop just short of the floor and hover your leg as far forward as possible in a slow controlled manner, while keeping your back flat to the floor and your core engaged

4. Tap the floor with your heel, pause slightly, and then return your leg to the start position in a slow controlled manner

5. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Leg Lowering - Slides
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs off the floor at 90-degrees. Place your arms straight by your side or on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drop one heel to the floor in a slow controlled manner. Then slide your heel as far forward as possible, making sure to keep your back flat to the floor and core engaged

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. When you straighten your leg, pause and then slowly return your leg to its starting position by reversing the movement

6. Repeat on the other side

name: Alternating March - Hips  90 Degrees
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor, heels on the floor, and both legs at 90-degrees. Place your arms straight by your side or on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lift one leg so your hip is at less than a 90-degree angle. As you raise your legs it’s important that you keep your back flat to the floor

4. When you reach the top, pause and then lift the other leg

5. Pause again and then lower your legs one after the other in a slow controlled manner. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower, keeping constant tension in your core

6. Reverse the order for the next rep

name: Alternating Neutral Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Take up a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Alternating Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

3. Keep on the toes for the back foot

4. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

5. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

6. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

7. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

8. Repeat with the opposite leg

name: Alternating Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine. Hands can be by your side or out in front for balance

3. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

4. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

5. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

6. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

7. Repeat on the other side

name: Ankle Banded Lateral Walk
description:
1. Place a band just above your ankles and place your feet wide enough apart to create tension in the band

2. With a neutral spine, hinge at the hips slightly by pushing your bum back, your knees should be in line with your toes and slightly bent

3. Interlock your hands out in front to help with balance

4. From here, while bracing your core, take a step to the side by stepping with the foot

5. Your foot shouldn't come too high off the floor when you step. Land with your foot flat, keeping your weight centered over the middle of your foot

6. As you step, your foot and knee should stay aligned, so as you move your knee is stacked directly on top of your foot

7. The other leg should stationary, making sure to keep the knee aligned over the foot and resist and inward movement. This leg provides resistance by stretching the band

8. Once you've planted your foot, step across with the stationary leg so you end up in the starting position

9. Step across for desired reps and then repeat going the opposite direction

name: Assisted Ab Roller
description:
1. Position yourself far enough away from a wall or a heavy object such as a dumbbell or weight plate on the floor

2. Then set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine

3. Grab the handles of the ab roller with both hands and position it on the floor underneath your chest. Your arms should be straight but not locked out

4. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

5. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then simultaneously push the roller and extend your hips forward in a slow controlled manner

6. As you move forward your torso will lower towards the floor but ensure your hips stay level. Your hips shouldn’t drop towards the floor and be careful not to extend your spine

6. Keep your head neutral and looking at the floor, making sure to keep a neutral spine and constant tension in your core

7. Keep moving until the roller reaches the object in front, pause slightly, and then return to the start position by pulling the roller back towards you. Your torso should raise up as you pull in towards yourself

8. Be careful to never pull the roller underneath yourself

name: Assisted Back Extension
description:
1. Get into a prone position on the floor, lying on your stomach with your legs straight and feet together

2. Your head should be facing the floor and your hands flat on the floor by the side of your head. Ensure you have a neutral spine and your shoulder blades are pulled back

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your chest from the floor in a controlled manner while keeping your hands on the floor to assist the movement

4. Go as high as you can while maintaining the correct form. As you raise up ensure you avoid any movement in your legs and upper back

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor throughout the movement

6. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lower back and glutes

name: Assisted Leg Lowering
description:
1. Lie on your back with your legs out straight.

2. Place a band around one foot and hold it with your hands.

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise your two legs (keeping them relaxed) as high as possible without the knee bending

4. Start the movement by engaging your core and lowering the free leg as low as you can, maintaining the position with the other leg.

5. Pause slightly and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core

6. Keep a neutral spine throughout

name: Assisted Leg Lowering To Bolster.
description:
1. Lie on your back with the back of your legs on a foam roller, just above the ankle

2. Place a band around one of your feet and grasp with both hands

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise the leg with the band (keeping it relaxed) as high as possible without the knee bending

4. Start the movement by engaging your core and raising the other leg to the same level as the assisted leg

5. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core as you lower your leg

6. Ensure you maintain the position of the assisted leg as you lower the other leg. Keep a neutral spine throughout

7. Gently touch the roller and repeat

name: B-Stance Barbell Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box, or aerobic step so it won’t slide backward. Sit in the front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place it over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

3. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Then straighten one leg, placing your heel just in front of the other foot, and point your toes in the air

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If the chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

7. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: B-Stance Barbell Hip Thrust with Mini Band
description:
1. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

2. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

4. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Then straighten one leg, placing your heel just in front of the other foot and point your toes in the air

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

8. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

9. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: B-Stance Barbell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart behind a barbell

2. Grab the barbell just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully deadlift the bar up or lift it up off a rack

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. Get into a b-stance position by taking a small step back with one leg. Slightly bend at the knee with the back leg and go onto your toes. The front foot should be flat and knee soft

5. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

6. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

7. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

8. Make sure to keep the front knee soft and avoid locking it out

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: B-Stance BB Glute Bridge w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your legs straight

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

4. Then bring your feet in so they’re flat together along your centre line and grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

5. Get into your b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through you the heel of the foot closest to your body. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

7. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: B-Stance Bosu Ball BB Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on your back with your head propped up on a Bosu ball and your legs straight

2. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

3. Then bring your feet in so they’re flat together along your centre line and grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

4. Get into your b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through you the heel of the foot closest to your body. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

6. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: B-Stance DB Glute Bridge w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

4. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

5. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a dumbbell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

7. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line

2. Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: B-Stance Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line

2. Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

6. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

name: B-Stance Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

3. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Then straighten one leg, placing your heel just in front of the other foot and point your toes in the air

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

7. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

8. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: B-Stance Hip Thrust w Mini Band
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

name: B-Stance Kettlebell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line

2. Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

6. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Band 2 Rear Foot Elevated Single-Leg RDL
Band 2 Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
Band Around Hips - Plank
description:
1. Anchor a band and then position it around your hips

2. Then set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Then go onto your forearms and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

4. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

7. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Band Assisted Chin Up.
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a chin-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a chin-up bar with shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Assisted Close Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a chin-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc and place one foot in the band. Grasp a chin-up bar so your hands are inside of your shoulders, palms facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Assisted Close Grip Pull Up.
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a pull-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a pull up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside of shoulder-width apart

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Assisted Neutral Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a chin-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a neutral grip chin-up bar with both hands so your palms are facing each other

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Assisted Pull Up
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a pull-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a pull up bar with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Assisted Reverse Nordic Curl
description:
1. Anchor a band to a stable surface such as a rack or cable machine. Grasp with both hands so your arms are straight

2. Then assume a full kneeling position on the floor, with your knees on a mat

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then slowly lower yourself backward in a slow controlled manner, using the band as assistance to lower yourself

4. Keep your hips extended and make sure you keep a neutral spine as you lower. Your body position from your knees to your head shouldn't change at any time during the movement

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause slightly and then reverse the movement by contracting your quads and pushing forward

6. Don't pull on the band as you come up, making sure to keep your arms straight

7. As you move forward, make sure your hips stay extended and your back straight

name: Band Assisted Standard Dip
description:
1. Place a band around a dip bar to begin

2. Stand on a step with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart on a dip bar

3. Your legs should be straight with your feet slightly behind you on the step. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft

4. Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine and that your shoulders are depressed down

5. Start by lifting your feet from the step and placing your two knees (just at the top of the shin) on the band, holding your weight with the help of the band

6. Then begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, using the band to help you lower. Maintain constant tension in your triceps

7. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

8. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

9. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

10. Be careful not to sway forward or back in the band, keeping your movement straight up and down

name: Band Assisted Wide Grip Pull Up.
description:
1. Anchor a band around the middle of a pull-up bar

2. Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a pull up bar with an overhand grip and place them wider than shoulder-width apart

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

5. Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band B-Stance Glute Bridge
description:
1. Position a band under each foot and then pull the band over the front of your thighs. Then lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your center line

2. Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

6. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Band B-Stance RDL
description:
1. Anchor a band around one foot and grasp the end with the opposite hand. Place your arms straight by your side

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Get into a b-stance position by taking a small step back with one leg. Slightly bend at the knee with the back leg and go onto your toes. The front foot should be flat and knee soft

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. Make sure to keep the front knee soft and avoid locking it out

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Band Behind Head Pull Apart
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and position a band over the back of your neck/upper back

2. Grab the band in each hand with an overhand grip with your arms straight in front of you. Ensure you have enough tension in the band to begin

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and bringing your arms to the side, pulling the band apart. As you pull the band externally rotate your hands so your palm is facing away from you

5. Pull the band across your chest and pause slightly, squeezing your shoulder blades

6. Keep your chest up while maintaining a neutral spine

7. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the rear of your shoulders, upper back, and core

name: Band Bent Over Row - Neutral
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with a neutral grip, palms facing towards the midline of your body

2. Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Bent Over Row - Pronated
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with an overhand grip, palms facing towards the body

2. Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Bent Over Row - Supinated
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with an underhand grip, palms facing away from the body

2. Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Bicep Curl.
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp with both hands. Place your arms straight by your side with your palms facing forwards

2. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Band Both Hands B-Stance RDL
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and grasp the ends with both hands. Place your arms straight by your side

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Get into a b-stance position by taking a small step back with one leg. Slightly bend at the knee with the back leg and go onto your toes. The front foot should be flat and knee soft

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. Make sure to keep the front knee soft and avoid locking it out

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Band Both Hands Single-Leg RDL
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and position your feet hip-width apart. Grasp both ends of the band with your hands

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the free leg off the floor. At the same time lower the band in a controlled manner

5. Your torso, the band, and your back leg should all move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

6. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine

7. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

8. Lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

10. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Band Crunch
description:
1. Place a band around your back and under your arms, grasping with each hand. Then lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat and arms straight in the air

2. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned

3. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then raise your upper back and shoulders from the floor while pushing the band in the air at the same time

4. Keep your torso rigid and your core braced

5. Think about raising your chest to the ceiling rather than just crunching towards your knees

6. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core

7. Avoid any jerking movements with your head or using your arms for momentum

name: Band Elbow Side Plank
description:
1. Anchor a band and then position it around your hips

2. Then lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched. There should be no slack in the band

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

5. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

6. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line

name: Band Floor Press
description:
1. Take up a seated position on the floor and wrap a band around your shoulder blades, grabbing an end with each hand

2. Retract your shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push your hands up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor

3. Begin by slowly lowering your hands while keeping them at a 45-degree angle to your body until your elbows touch the floor

4. While keeping your back flat to the floor, proceed to push your feet through the floor and drive your hands back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top

5. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core

name: Band Front Squat
description:
1. Place a band under both feet and grasp with both hands. Then bring the band into a front rack position by bringing your elbows forward so your upper arm is parallel with the floor and both hands are in front of your shoulders

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Band Frontal Raise
description:
1. Stand on a band with your feet shoulder-width apart and grab the band in each hand with your arms by your side. Don’t rest your hands on the front of your thighs

2. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

3. Begin by bracing your core, then raise the band in front of you by bringing your arms forward, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your shoulders at any point

4. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the front of your shoulders and core

5. The band shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Band Glute Bridge
description:
1. Position a band under each foot and then pull the band over the front of your thighs. Then lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

4. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

5. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

7. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Band Hammer Curl
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp with both hands. Place your arms straight by your side with your hands in a neutral grip, palms facing towards the midline of the body

2. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Band Hip Extension Barbell RDL
description:
1. Place a band at hip height on either side of a rack so it’s positioned across the front of your hips. You should be positioned behind the barbell facing out of the rack

2. Grab the barbell just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully lift the bar up from the rack and step forward, planting your feet hip-width apart

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward against the band and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Band Lateral Raise
description:
1. Stand on a band with your feet shoulder-width apart and position it over the back of your neck/upper back

2. Grab the band in each hand with your arms by your side. Don’t rest your hands on your side

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and raising your arms to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your elbows at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the side of your shoulders and core

6. Hands shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Band Lateral Walk
description:
1. Place a band just above your knees and place your feet hip-width apart, just enough to create some tension in the band

2. With a neutral spine, hinge at the hips slightly by pushing your bum back, your knees should be in line with your toes and slightly bent

3. Interlock your hands out in front to help with balance

4. From here, while bracing your core, take a step to the side by stepping with the foot and pushing your knee against the band

5. Your foot shouldn't come too high off the floor when you step. Land with your foot flat, keeping your weight centered over the middle of your foot

6. As you step, your foot and knee should stay aligned, so as you move your knee is stacked directly on top of your foot

7. The other leg should stationary, making sure to keep the knee aligned over the foot and resist any inward movement. This leg provides resistance by stretching the band

8. Once you've planted your foot, step across with the stationary leg so you end up in the starting position

9. Step across for desired reps and then repeat going the opposite direction

name: Band Loaded Chin Up
description:
1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a chin-up bar with a shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Loaded Close Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a chin-up bar so your hands are inside of your shoulders, palms facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Loaded Close Grip Pull Up
description:
1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside shoulder width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Loaded Neutral Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a neutral grip chin-up bar with both hands so your palms are facing each other. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Loaded Pull Up
description:
1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Loaded Wide Grip Pull Up
description:
