1. Anchor a band to heavy dumbbells, kettlebells, or a squat rack and attach it to a weight belt around your waist

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place it wider than shoulder-width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Band Lying Leg Curl
description:
1. Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band around both legs

2. Your legs should be straight and there should be no slack in the band

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by pulling your heels in towards your bum in a controlled manner and squeeze your hamstrings. Movement should come solely from bending your knees

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause, and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your hamstrings

6. If you find that you can’t keep your spine neutral, you can place a pad/mat underneath your stomach to help with your position

7. Repeat

name: Band Lying Single-Leg Curl
description:
1. Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band around one leg

2. Your legs should be straight and there should be no slack in the band

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by pulling your heel in towards your bum in a controlled manner and squeeze your hamstring. Movement should come solely from bending your knee

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause, and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your hamstring

6. If you find that you can’t keep your spine neutral, you can place a pad/mat underneath your stomach to help with your position

7. Repeat

name: Band Over Shoulder Split Squat
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and over the same side shoulder. You can hold the band secure with your hand if needed

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, and glutes

name: Band Overhead Triceps Extension
description:
1. Start by positioning a band under both feet. With the band behind you, grasp with both hands and place them behind your head

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your core engaged

3. Place your hands in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

4. Elbows should be shoulder-width apart. Keep this position for the entire movement, preventing the elbows flare out

5. Begin the movement by pushing the band up with both hands and extending your arms. Don't lock out your elbows at the top

6. From this position pause slightly and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in the triceps and core

name: Band Pull Aparts
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and grab a band in each hand with an overhand grip, arms straight in front of you. Ensure you have enough tension in the band to begin

2. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

3. Begin by bracing your core and bringing your arms to the side, pulling the band apart. As you pull the band externally rotate your hands so your palm is facing away from you

4. Pull the band across your chest and pause slightly, squeezing your shoulder blades

5. Keep your chest up while maintaining a neutral spine

6. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the rear of your shoulders, upper back, and core

name: Band Quadruped Kickback (Band Underhand)
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Position a band under one of your hands and grasp the other end of the band with the opposite hand

4. Bring this arm parallel with your torso and then place it at a ninety-degree angle

5. Start the movement by pushing the band backwards and extending your arm, ensuring you keep your upper arm stationary at all times

6. Stop just short of locking out your elbow and pause slightly, contracting your triceps. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your triceps and core

7. Keep a neutral spine throughout, making sure to stop any rotation in your torso

name: Band Rear Foot Elevated Single-Leg RDL
description:
1. Anchor a band around one foot and grab the band with the opposite hand. Elevate your other foot behind you

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lowering the band in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep your back straight throughout

5. Your torso and the band should move at the same time

6. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine

7. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

8. Lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Band Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Anchor a band around one foot and grasp with the opposite hand. Elevate your other foot behind you

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes either pointed down or laces flat against the surface

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, and glutes

name: Band Reverse Curl
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp with both hands with an overhand grip. Place your arms straight by your side with your palms facing backward

2. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension in your biceps and forearms, keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Band Seated Face Pull
description:
1. Sat on the floor, place a band around both feet and grasp with both hands with an overhand grip

2. Sit with an upright posture while maintaining a neutral spine for the entire movement, eyes focused looking forward

3. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the band in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

4. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the band apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Seated Neutral Row
description:
1. Sat on the floor with both legs straight, place a band around both feet and grasp an end with each hand with a neutral grip, your palms facing towards the midline of your body

2. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

3. Start by bracing your core and then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands in towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

4. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Seated One Arm Row
description:
1. Sat on the floor with both legs straight, anchor a band around one foot, and then grasp the end of the band in the opposite hand with a neutral grip, your palms facing towards the midline of your body

2. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

3. Start by bracing your core and then retract your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the band in towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

4. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Seated Pronated Row
description:
1. Sat on the floor with both legs straight, place a band around both feet and grasp an end with each hand with an overhand grip, your palms facing towards the floor

2. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

3. Start by bracing your core and then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands in towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

4. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Seated Supinated Row
description:
1. Sat on the floor with both legs straight, place a band around both feet and grasp an end with each hand with an underhand grip, your palms facing up

2. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

3. Start by bracing your core and then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands in towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

4. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Single Arm Standing Chest Press
description:
1. While standing, wrap a band around your back and grasp an end with each hand. Reduce the slack so one side has more tension

2. Begin by getting into an upright posture with a neutral spine, with your shoulders depressed down and shoulder blades squeezed

3. Start by pressing the band straight in front so your arm is at a 45-degree angle to your body

4. Be careful not to rotate your torso during the movement and ensure the shoulder remains down

5. Finish just short of locking out your elbow and then pause slightly

6. Then proceed to reverse the movement by bringing the band back to the start position, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core

name: Band Single-Leg RDL
description:
1. Anchor a band around one foot and position your feet hip-width apart. Grab the other end with the opposite hand

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the band off the floor. At the same time lower the band in a controlled manner

5. Your torso, the band, and your back leg should all move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

6. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine

7. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

8. Lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

10. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Band Split Squat.
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and grasp the ends with both hands

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, and glutes

name: Band Split Stance Single Arm Curl
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and grasp with the same side hand. Place your arm straight by your side with your palms facing forwards

2. Get into a split stance by stepping forward with the opposite foot and slightly bending your knee. You can use the free hand to brace against your leg

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arm locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension in your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Band Split Stance Single Arm Hammer Curl
description:
1. Position a band under one foot and grasp with the same side hand. Place your arm straight by your side with a neutral grip, palm facing in towards the midline of your body

2. Get into a split stance by stepping forward with the opposite foot and slightly bending your knee. You can use the free hand to brace against your leg

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arm locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension in your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Band Split Stance Single Arm Row
description:
1. In a split stance, position a band under both feet and grasp the middle of the band with one hand in a neutral grip, palm facing towards the midline of your body

2. Stand tall with your arm straight by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the band up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Band Split Stance Single Triceps Extension
description:
1. Start by positioning a band under one foot and grasping the end with the hand on the same side. With the band behind you, bring your hand behind your head

2. Then split your stance by stepping forward with the opposite foot and slightly bending your knee. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your core engaged

3. Place your hand in a neutral position, palm facing in towards the midline of your body

4. Elbow should be shoulder-width apart. Keep this position for the entire movement, preventing the elbow from flaring out

5. Begin the movement by pushing the band up with your hand and extending your arm. Don't lock out your elbow at the top

6. From this position pause slightly and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in the triceps and core

name: Band Squat
description:
1. Place a band under both feet and position it over the base of the rear of your neck neck

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your body weight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Band Standing Chest Press
description:
1. While standing, wrap a band around your back and grasp an end with each hand

2. Begin by getting into an upright posture with a neutral spine, with your shoulders depressed down and shoulder blades squeezed

3. Start by pressing the band straight in front so your arms are at a 45-degree angle to your body

4. Bring your hands in towards each other as your press the band forward, but don't let them tocuh

5. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and then pause slightly

6. Then proceed to reverse the movement by bringing the band back to the start position, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core

name: Band Straight Arm Pulldown
description:
1. Anchor a band positioned above your head

2. Grasp the band with a neutral grip, (palms facing each other) roughly shoulder-width apart. Arms should be straight, but not locked out

3. Grip the band loosely so your back muscles are doing the work and not just your arms

4. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine, then hinge at the hips and lean forward slightly

5. Begin the movement by depressing your shoulder blades and pulling the band towards your thighs, pausing when you reach full contraction in your Lats

6. Return to the start position in a slow controlled manner by raising your arms back, keeping constant tension in your Lats and core

name: Band Straight Arm Side Plank
description:
1. Anchor a band and then position it around your hips

2. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

5. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

6. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight

7. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes. The aim is to stop yourself rotating forward with the band

name: Band Upright Row.
description:
1. Stand on a band with your feet shoulder-width apart and grab the band in each hand with your arms straight in front of your thighs

2. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

3. Begin by bracing your core, then pull the band by driving your elbows up, ensuring you keep the band close to the body as you do so

4. Bring the band towards shoulder height, with your arm now parallel to the floor

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your shoulders and core

name: Banded Barbell Deadlift
description:
1. Anchor down resistance bands on either end of a barbell and place over the ends. Use the hooks on a platform or rack to secure the bands, or use heavy dumbbells/kettlebells

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Banded Barbell RDL
description:
1. Anchor down two bands on either end of a barbell and place over the top. You can use heavy dumbbells or kettlebells to keep them in place, or a rack with hooks

2. Start with your feet hip-width apart behind a barbell

3. Grab the barbell just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully deadlift the bar up

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

5. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

6. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

7. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

8. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Banded Bird Dog
description:
1. Place a band around your foot and grab the other end with the opposite hand

2. Then set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

4. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending the leg with the band backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

5. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

6. Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

7. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

8. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

9. Repeat

name: Banded Bird Dog - Elevated
description:
1. Place a band around your foot and grab the other end with the opposite hand

2. Then set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

4. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending the leg with the band backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

5. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

6. Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

7. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

8. Return to the start in a slow controlled manner, this time keeping your hand and knee off the floor. Ensure you keep constant tension in your core

9. Repeat

name: Banded Bird Dog - Isometric
description:
1. Place a band around your foot and grab the other end with the opposite hand

2. Then set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

4. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending the leg with the band backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

5. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

6. Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

7. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

8. Maintain this position for the allotted time, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Banded Clamshell
description:
1. Place a band around your knees and lie on your side. Support your head with your hand and place your other hand flat on the floor or on your hips

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. With your legs straight bring one foot up and rest it on the back of your calf, your top knee should be on top of and slightly in front of the bottom knee

4. Begin the movement by posteriorly tilting your pelvis (tucking in your tailbone), then drop your knee towards the floor in a controlled manner by internally rotating your hip, using your foot to swivel on your calf

5. Then reverse the movement by externally rotating your hip and raising your knee by pushing against the band, going just high enough so that your hips don't move backward

6. Pause slightly at the top and then repeat, ensuring the movement only comes from internally and externally rotating your hip

name: Banded Dead Bug
description:
1. Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band behind you

2. Grab the band with each hand and position your arms straight in the air above your eye line, with both legs at 90-degree angles

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Banded Fire Hydrants
description:
1. Place a band just above the top of your knees

2. Get into a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, making sure the spine is in a neutral position

4. Begin the movement by rotating one of your legs out to the side at a 90-degree angle and pushing the band

5. As you move it's important that the movement only comes from externally rotating your hip upwards

6. Don't rock or lean to the opposite side when you raise your leg as doing so will result in you using momentum to raise your leg and take away from the target muscle working correctly

7. Make sure your torso and the downed leg stays stationary and your spine neutral throughout

8. Go as high as your range of motion allows you to, then pause slightly and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Banded Good Mornings
description:
1. Stand on a band with your hip shoulder-width apart and position it over the back of your neck/upper back

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower your torso in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

5. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and standing back up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, and hamstrings

name: Banded Knee Bent Dead Bug
description:
1. Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band behind you

2. Grab the band with each hand and position your arms straight in the air above your eye line, with both legs at 90-degree angles

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lower one foot towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Banded Landmine RDL
description:
1. While facing the landmine, position your feet about hip-width apart with toes facing forwards

2. Place the ends of the band under each foot and place the middle of the band over the barbell

3. Carefully lift the bar up or have a partner hand it to you. While standing tall, grab the end of the barbell with both hands. Either clasp your hands together or have one behind the other

4. Keep the arms straight so the bar is positioned just in front of the legs

5. From here, while bracing your core and engaging your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits), begin by hinging at the hips

6. Lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them

8. From the bottom position, drive the hips forward and bring the bar back up to the starting position while maintaining constant tension through the core, glutes, and upper back

name: Banded Lateral Distraction Hip Capsule Mobilisation
Banded Psoas March
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor and loop a band around both feet. Then position both legs at 90-degree angles and place your hands on your hips

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor in a slow controlled manner. The other foot should remain stationary throughout

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. Gently touch the floor with your heel, pause, and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

7. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Banded Push Up
description:
1. Put each hand in either end of a resistance band and place it around your back so it wraps around the upper back area

2. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

3. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

4. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

5. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

6. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

7. Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

8. From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

name: Banded RDL
description:
1. Position a band under both feet and grasp with both hands. Feet should be hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

5. The band should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

6. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Banded Single-Arm Upside Down KB Floor Press
Banded Spanish Squat
description:
1. Anchor a thick band around a secure surface at knee height and then position each leg in the band just below the knee. Step far enough back so you create enough tension in the band, as this will support you during the movement

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Banded Trap Bar Deadlift
description:
1. Anchor down resistance bands on either end of a barbell and place over the ends. Use the hooks on a platform or rack to secure the bands, or use heavy dumbbells/kettlebells

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. Stand in the middle of the trap bar, with the middle of your foot in line with the center of the handles (this helps with balance)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side
5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, lifting the bar off the floor. Keep the bar path in as straight a vertical line as possible)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar travel down in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Barbell Back Squat.
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

3. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

4. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

5. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

6. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

8. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

9. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

12. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Bench Press.
description:
1. Sat at the end of a bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

2. Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands. Your legs should be at not more than a 90-degree angle with your feet flat

3. Next, pinch your shoulder blades together and push your chest out. You should then have a slight arch in your lower back with your upper back pressed against the bench

4. Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the middle of your chest, this is your starting position

5. Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

6. The bar should finish at the base of your sternum (breastbone), either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

7. Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

8. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

9. Ensure correct starting position and repeat

name: Barbell Box Front Squat
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Angle a box a step back from the rack so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

3. Grasp the bar shoulder width and step towards the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the front of your shoulders/collarbones. Push your elbows up from here, with your upper arm parallel to the ground. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

4. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack and position the box between your feet

5. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

6. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

7. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

8. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly and bounce off the box

9. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible, making sure to keep your elbows up

10. When you touch the box reverse the movement straightaway by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

11. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

12. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

13. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Box Squat
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Angle a box a step back from the rack so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

3. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

4. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack and position the box between your feet

5. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

6. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

7. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

8. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly and bounce off the box

9. When you touch the box reverse the movement straightaway by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

12. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell California Press
description:
1. Sat at the end of a bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

2. Grab the bar shoulder-width apart with both hands. Your legs should be at not more than a 90-degree angle with your feet flat

3. Next, pinch your shoulder blades together and push your chest out. You should then have a slight arch in your lower back with your upper back pressed against the bench

4. Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned directly over your throat, this is your starting position

5. Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows forward and bringing the bar towards your throat

6. Bring the bar as low as your ability allows you and then return to the start position, keeping constant tension in your triceps

7. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Barbell Curl
description:
1. Grab barbell around shoulder width apart with an underhand grip.

2. With your elbows tucked in by your side, curl the bar up by bending your elbows and lifting it towards your forehead.

3. The rest of your body should remind as stationary as possible throughout.

4. Curl the bar to a position that enables you to maintain constant tension, think about 90%. Any higher and you will lose tension on the biceps.

5. Pause and squeeze at the top, then return to the start position in a controlled manner.

6. For bonus points, bring the elbows slightly forward at the top of the movement. This flexes the shoulder slightly and will increase the tension on the biceps.

name: Barbell Deadlift.
description:
1. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

2. The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

3. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

4. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

5. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

6. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

7. Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

8. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

9. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

10. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

11. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

12. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Barbell Frog Pump
description:
1. Lie on your back with your legs straight

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

4. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and bring your feet in and position your heels together

5. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through the outside of your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

7. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Barbell Frog Pump w Mini Band
description:
1. Lie on your back with your legs straight and position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

4. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and bring your feet in and position your heels together

5. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through the outside of your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

7. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout, making sure to push out against the band at all times

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Barbell Front Squat.
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Grasp the bar shoulder width and step towards the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the front of your shoulders/collarbones. Push your elbows up from here, with your upper arm parallel to the ground. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

3. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

4. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

5. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

6. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

8. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible, making sure to keep your elbows up

9. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

12. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your legs straight

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

4. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and then bring your feet in

5. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

6. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar and push through your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

8. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

9. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Barbell Glute Bridge w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your legs straight

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

5. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and then bring your feet in

6. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

7. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

8. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar and push through your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

9. Drive the knees out against the band. Knees should track your middle toes throughout, and your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

10. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

11. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, continuing to push your knees out against the band while keeping constant tension in the glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

3. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Barbell Hip Thrust with Mini Band
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

4. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out against the band and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Barbell Overhead Press.
description:
1. Adjust the rack position so the barbell is just below shoulder height

2. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab the bar just outside of shoulder width

3. Position the bar just below the collarbones with your elbows tucked in and pointed down

4. Carefully step back from the rack and plant your feet in the ground

5. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, press the bar to lockout overhead by extending your legs

6. Make sure the barbell finishes directly above the head, with the biceps in line with the ears

7. Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

8. Proceed to lower the bar back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Barbell Paused Back Squat
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

3. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

4. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

5. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

6. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

8. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

9. When you reach the bottom of the squat pause for 1-2 seconds and then return to standing by pushing through both feet and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

12. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Paused Box Squat
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Angle a box a step back from the rack so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

3. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

4. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack and position the box between your feet

5. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

6. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

7. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

8. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you don’t hit the box with force

9. Once sat, relax the leg and hip muscles and pause for one or two seconds. It’s important that you keep your spine stable by keeping a rigid torso

10. Stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

11. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

12. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

13. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Paused Deadlift
description:
1. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

2. The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

3. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

4. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

5. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

6. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

7. Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

8. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs

9. When you've lifted the bar to just below the knee, pause for the allotted time and then pull the bar the rest of the way up. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Barbell Push Press
description:
1. Adjust the rack position so the barbell is just below shoulder height

2. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab the bar just outside of shoulder width

3. Position the bar just below the collarbones with your elbows tucked in and pointed down

4. Carefully step back from the rack and plant your feet in the ground

5. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, dip slightly at the knees and hips, then press the bar to lockout overhead by extending your legs and arms simultaneously

6. Make sure barbell finishes directly above the head, with the biceps in line with the ears

7. Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

8. Proceed to lower the bar back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Barbell Rack Pull
description:
1. Set up a barbell just above knee height on the safety columns of a rack

2. Start with your feet hip-width apart behind the barbell

3. Grab the barbell just wider than shoulder-width apart. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. You should have a neutral spine and the barbell should be lined up over the middle of your foot

5. While keeping this tension throughout your body, begin the movement by pulling the bar up from the rack in as vertical a line as possible and extending your hips forward

6. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot, ensuring you keep constant tension in your Lats, upper back, and core

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by pushing the hips backward and lowering the barbell back to the safety columns. Make sure to keep your shoulder retracted and spine neutral as you lower the weight

name: Barbell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart behind a barbell

2. Grab the barbell just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully deadlift the bar up or lift it up off a rack

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Barbell Reverse Lunge.
description:
1. Position a barbell so its chest height in a rack or have a partner carefully lift the barbell on your back

2. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

3. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

4. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

5. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

6. Keep on the toes for the back foot

7. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

8. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

9. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

10. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Barbell Rocking Box Squat
description:
1. Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

2. Angle a box a step back from the rack so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

3. Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

4. Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack and position the box between your feet

5. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

6. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

7. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

8. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

9. Once sat, rock back by bringing your torso into an upright position, then lean forward and stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. When you rock back its important that you don’t relax all your muscles, particularly your core

11. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

12. The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

13. Pause and repeat

name: Barbell Suitcase Hold
description:
1. Stand in a rack with a barbell on the safety bars so it’s just above your knee. Stand side on and position yourself at the middle of the barbell

2. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

3. Grab the middle of the bar with your hand and ensure a tight grip

4. Begin by bracing your core and standing tall with the barbell. Keep the bar from touching your side and ensure you don’t dip to the side at any point
