5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Aim to keep the bar as parallel to the floor as possible

name: Bench Dip - Feet Elevated
description:
1. Sit on a side of a flat bench or box with a step/box/bench in front of you

2. Place your hands just wider than shoulder-width apart on the edge

3. Lift yourself off by taking a step forward with both feet and elevating your feet on the step in front

4. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft. Retract your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders depressed down

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, keeping your torso close to the bench as you move and maintaining constant tension in your triceps

5. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

6. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

7. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

8. Keep the weight on your hands and not your legs as you move, rather using them to push you yourself up and not your legs

name: Bench Dip - Knees Bent
description:
1. Sit on a side of a flat bench or box

2. Place your hands just wider than shoulder-width apart on the edge

3. Lift yourself off by taking a step forward with both feet and bringing your hips forward. The further away the greater the difficulty

4. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft. Retract your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders depressed down

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, keeping your torso close to the bench as you move and maintaining constant tension in your triceps

5. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

6. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

7. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

8. Keep the weight on your hands and not your legs as you move, rather using them to push you yourself up and not your legs

name: Bench Dip - Plate On Legs
description:
1. Sit on a side of a flat bench or box with a step/box/bench in front of you

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner place a plate on your thighs (this can also be done by a partner when you reach step 4)

3. Place your hands just wider than shoulder-width apart on the edge

4. Lift yourself off by taking a step forward with both feet and elevating your feet on the step in front

5. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft. Retract your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders depressed down

6. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, keeping your torso close to the bench as you move and maintaining constant tension in your triceps

7. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

8. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears and that you keep the plate carefully balanced on your thighs

9. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

10. Keep the weight on your hands and not your legs as you move, rather using them to push you yourself up and not your legs

name: Bench Dip.
description:
1. Sit on a side of a flat bench or box

2. Place your hands just wider than shoulder-width apart on the edge

3. Lift yourself off by taking a step forward with both feet and straightening your legs

4. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft. Retract your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders depressed down

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, keeping your torso close to the bench as you move and maintaining constant tension in your triceps

5. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

6. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

7. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

8. Keep the weight on your hands and not your legs as you move, rather using them to push you yourself up and not your legs

name: Bench Single-Leg Squat
description:
1. Stand in front of a bench or box. The bench/box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat

2. Start with your feet side by side, then slightly raise one foot from the floor by bending your leg. Your foot should be pointed forward

3. Your body weight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knee in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Keep your knee in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knee, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly and bounce off the box

8. When you touch the box reverse the movement straightaway by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knee out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Bench Single-Leg Squat With Pause
description:
1. Stand in front of a bench or box. The bench/box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat

2. Start with your feet side by side, then slightly raise one foot from the floor by bending your leg. Your foot should be pointed forward

3. Your body weight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knee in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Keep your knee in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knee, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly and bounce off the box

8. When you touch the box pause slightly and then reverse the movement by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knee out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Bent Over Cable French Press
description:
1. With your back to a cable machine, have a partner hand you or carefully grab a rope

2. Bend your elbows and bring the rope behind your head, upper arm next to your ears. The end of the rope should be touching, and your palms should be facing each other

3. Split your stance and bend over to a 45-degree angle, keeping your spine in a neutral position and your core engaged

4. The pulley should have a slight downward angle to ensure constant tension on the triceps

5. Begin by extending your arms and pushing the rope out in front util your arms are straight, but not locked out fully

6. Ensure your upper arms stay fixed throughout the entire movement

7. Pause and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Bird Dog - Arm Only
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg arm out in front until it’s straight

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

6. Repeat

name: Bird Dog - Elevated
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

6. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

7. Pause and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, this time keeping your hand and knee off the floor. Ensure you keep constant tension in your core

8. Repeat

name: Bird Dog - Elevated Leg Only
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg backwards until it’s straight

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Think about pushing your heel backwards as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

6. Pause and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, this time keeping your knee off the floor. Ensure you keep constant tension in your core

7. Repeat

name: Bird Dog - Knee To Elbow
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

6. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

7. Pause and then bring your elbow and knee underneath your body in a slow controlled manner until they touch, ensuring you keep a neutral spine during the entire movement

9. Return to the start in a slow controlled manner, this time keeping your hand and knee off the floor. Ensure you keep constant tension in your core

10. Repeat

6. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

7. Repeat

6. Maintain this position for the allotted time, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

7. Repeat on the other side

name: Bird Dog - Leg Slide
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core, then extend your leg by sliding your foot backward along the floor while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front. Finish with both straight

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

6. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

7. Repeat

name: Bird Dog - Leg Slide + Elevate Only
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and extending your leg by sliding your foot backward along the floor

4. When the leg is fully extended backward, pause briefly and then raise the foot from the floor while keeping your leg straight.

4. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

5. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

6. Repeat

description:

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg by sliding your foot backward along the floor while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front

4. When the leg is fully extended backward, pause briefly and then raise the foot from the floor while keeping your leg straight

5. The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

6. Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

6. Repeat

name: Bird Dog Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
description:
1. Set up in a bird dog position on a bench, with the dumbbell in the hand which is the same side as the knee on the bench

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and bringing the dumbbell up by driving the elbow back

4. Pull the dumbbell up towards the body, in a slight arcing motion, until your elbow is level (or just past) with your torso. The dumbbell should finish towards your hip with your arm at a ninety-degree angle

5. At the top of the movement, pause and squeeze your back muscles

6. Proceed to then reverse the movement and lower the weight in a slow, controlled manner

7. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, upper back, and core

8. Keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back as you move the dumbbell

name: Bird Dog.
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

name: Bodyweight Box Paused Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

8. Once sat, relax the leg and hip muscles and pause for one or two seconds. It’s important that you keep your spine stable by keeping a rigid torso

9. Stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. Pause and repeat

name: Bodyweight Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close, if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your body weight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the box. Be careful not to descend to quickly and bounce off the box

8. When you touch the box reverse the movement straightaway by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Bodyweight Box Squat at Home
description:
1. Stand 6-12 inches in front of a piece of furniture such as a chair, bed or sofa

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your body weight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you touch the chair. If you struggle to sit all the way down with correct form, then add some height and find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Be careful not to descend to quickly and bounce off of the chair

8. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

9. When you touch the chair reverse the movement straightaway by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. Pause and repeat

name: Bodyweight Rocking Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

8. Once sat, rock back by bringing your torso into an upright position, then lean forward and stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. When you rock back its important that you don’t relax all your muscles, particularly your core

10. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

11. Pause and repeat

name: Bodyweight Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

2. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your body weight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

4. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Bodyweight Sumo Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet wider than your normal squat stance and slightly turn them out. Mobility varies in people so find a position that’s comfortable for you

2. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

4. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Bosu Ball Barbell Frog Pump
description:
1. Lie on your back with your head propped up on a Bosu ball and legs straight

2. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

3. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and bring your feet in and position your heels together

4. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through the outside of your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

6. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Bosu Ball Barbell Glute Bridge w Band
description:
1. Position a band around your knees and lay on your back with your head propped up on a bosu ball and your legs straight

2. Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centered over your hips

3. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and then bring your feet in

4. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

5. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar and push through your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

7. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Bosu Ball Frog Pump
description:
1. Lie on your back with your head propped up on a Bosu ball and your heels together

2. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

3. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

4. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the outside of your heels

5. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout

6. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Bosu Ball Frog Pump w Mini Band
description:
1. Lie on your back with your head propped up on a Bosu ball and your heels together

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the outside of your heels

7. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout, making sure to push out against the band at all times

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Cable Drag Curl
description:
1. Attach two handles to two cable pulleys positioned at the bottom and stand with your back to the machine

2. Pick up a handle with both hands with an underhand grip and take a step forward so your upper arms get dragged behind you

3. Split your stance and bend over to a 45-degree angle, keeping your spine in a neutral position and your core engaged

4. With your arms behind you, lock your upper arm into position. You shouldn’t lose this position for the entire movement

5. With your upper arms locked in position, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the handles in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the handles, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner by reversing the movement, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Cable Fly
description:
1. Adjust the pulleys on the cable machine to chest height or slightly above. The line of pull should be directly in line with the middle of your chest during the movement.

2. Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the exercise and engage your core muscles.

3. Keep your chest up and your shoulders back and down.

4. Starting with your arms out in front, bring your arms out to the side and control the weight backwards, stretching the chest.

5. As you move backwards, make sure to keep a bend in your elbows.

6. Stop when the handles are in line with the chest. This will depend on your shoulder range of motion and flexibility.

7. Return to the start position by trying to bring your biceps together, squeezing your chest the you reach the top.

name: Cable Pull Through.
description:
1. Position a rope just above knee height on a cable pulley

2. Grab the rope between your legs and step forward so your back is to the machine. Brace yourself and stand with your feet hip-width apart

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and letting the rope get pulled through your legs in a slow controlled manner. Push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum)

6. Your hands should travel as high up your legs as possible while moving through smoothly and keeping the knees soft

7. Your torso should lower towards the floor as your hips go backward, ensuring you maintain a neutral spine

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the rope back through, squeezing your glutes in the process

8. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Cable Reverse Straight Bar Curl
description:
1. Attach a straight bar to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the bar with an overhand grip

2. Position your arms straight by your side and fix your upper arms to your sides

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the bar up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and forearms, keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the bar, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps, forearms and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Cable Straight Bar Curl
description:
1. Attach a straight bar to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the bar with an underhand grip

2. Position your arms straight by your side and fix your upper arms to your sides

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the bar up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the bar, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Chest Supported Dual Cable Low Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45–60-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach two handles at the bottom

2. Sit with your chest against the bench and grab a handle in each hand, with your palms facing each other. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so your hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Dual Cable Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 75–90-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach two handles. Adjust the height so handles are just below your chest when sat

2. Sit with your chest against the bench and grab a handle in each hand, with your palms facing each other. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward, resting your forehead on the bench if needed

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so your hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Dumbbell Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45-degree angle and lay with your chest on your bench, using your feet to brace yourself

2. Pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand and have your arms hanging naturally by your side. Dumbbells at a 45-degree angle

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulderd blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the dumbbells up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Low Rope Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45–60-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach a rope to the bottom

2. Grab the rope with a neutral grip and stand with your chest against the bench. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the rope in towards the bottom of your chest in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Low Rope Row To Chest
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45–60-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach a rope to the bottom

2. Grab the rope with an overhand grip and stand with your chest against the bench. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the rope in towards your chest in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows out to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Pronated Barbell Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45-degree angle and lay with your chest on a bench, using your feet to brace yourself

2. Pick up or have a partner hand you a barbell and grab with an overhand grip wider than shoulder-width apart. Your arms hanging naturally by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side (aim to touch the bench each rep). Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Single Cable Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 75–90-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach a handle. Adjust the height so handles are just below your chest when sat

2. Sit with your chest against the bench and grab a handle with one hand, with your palm facing in. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward, resting your forehead on the bench if needed

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Single Low Cable Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45–60-degree angle in front of a cable machine and attach a handle to the bottom

2. Sit with your chest against the bench and grab a handle with one hand, with your palm facing in. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulder depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Chest Supported Supinated Barbell Row
description:
1. Position a bench at a 45-degree angle and lay with your chest on a bench, using your feet to brace yourself

2. Pick up or have a partner hand you a barbell and grab with an underhand grip just wider than shoulder-width apart. Your arms hanging naturally by your side

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulders blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side (aim to touch the bench each rep). Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Chest should stay on the bench at all times

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Childs Pose
description:
1. Get onto your hands and knees on the floor

2. You should have a neutral spine with your head facing the floor

3. While keeping your hands in position on the floor, start by pushing your bum towards your heels and sitting backwards

4. Push put your hands out on the floor as far as possible

5. Your should feel a stretch in your back and shoulders

6. Hold for allotted time

name: Chin Up.
description:
1. Grasp a chin-up bar with a shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

6. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Clamshell
description:
1. Lie on your side and support your head with your hand. Place your other hand flat on the floor or on your hips

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. With your legs straight bring one foot up and rest it on the back of your calf, your top knee should be on top of and slightly in front of the bottom knee

4. Begin the movement by posteriorly tilting your pelvis (tucking in your tailbone), then drop your knee towards the floor in a controlled manner by internally rotating your hip, using your foot to swivel on your calf

5. Then reverse the movement by externally rotating your hip and raising your knee, going just high enough so that your hips don't move backward

6. Pause slightly at the top and then repeat, ensuring the movement only comes from internally and externally rotating your hip

name: Close Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Grasp a chin-up bar so your hands are inside of your shoulders, palms facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

6. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Close Grip Pull Up.
description:
1. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside shoulder width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

6. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Close Grip Push Up
description:
1) Set up by placing your palms on the floor with a less than shoulder-width position (move closer depending on ability and shoulder mobility), knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

2) Extend one leg back at a time and assume a push up position with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

3) Grip the floor by twisting your hands in place

4) With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by retracting the shoulder blades and bending at the elbows

5) Keep your elbows tucked into your side and descend as low as possible without touching the floor

6) From this position drive your body back up to the starting point while keeping constant tension throughout the chest, core, and glutes

7) Don’t lock out the elbows at the top position

name: Contralateral Dumbbell Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

3. Pick up a dumbbell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Contralateral Kettlebell Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

3. Pick up a kettlebell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Copenhagen Plank
description:
1. Begin by lying on your side with your body perpendicular to the bench or elevated surface.

2. Position yourself in a regular side plank position.

3. Put the inside of your top foot on the bench, with your leg straight.

4. Push through your raised foot and forearm to lift your body from the floor.

5. You can keep the bottom leg bent or straight.

6. Aim to keep a straight line from head to foot.

name: Curtsy Lunge
description:
1) Start with feet hip-width apart and facing forward

2) Start by engaging your core and taking a step back at a 45-degree angle with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle. Keep on the toes with the back foot

3) Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

4) The front leg should also finish at a 90-degree angle, while the foot should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

5) Keep the knee of the front leg stationary and pointed forward over your toes throughout

6) From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot while maintaining an upright torso. Make sure to keep constant tension in the core throughout the movement

name: DB Low Trap Raises (Incline Bench)
description:
1. Position bench at 30 degree angle.

2. Lay face down on the bench with weight in each hand, arms straight down, palms facing each other.

3. Start the movement by first retracting and squeezing your shoulder blades.

4. Then raise your arms out to 10 & 2, turning your hands out slightly.

5. Hold at the top and squeeze.

6. Lower down under control, keeping the tension in your shoulder blade area.

name: Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your arms straight in the air

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the opposite arm to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

7. Repeat on the opposite sides

name: Dead Bug Hold
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your hands flat against your knees straight

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core

4. Then push both legs with your hands, using your core to resist any movement and stop your legs from moving

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and make sure to breath

6. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core throughout

name: Deadlift From Blocks
description:
1. Elevate the barbell with blocks, bumper plates, etc

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Deadmill Sprints
description:
1. Stand on a turned off treadmill

2. Grab the handles and begin to move the treadmill belt by driving your legs and starting to sprint.

3. Stay nice and low and continue to power your legs as hard as you can for allotted time.

name: Decline Alternating Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set up on a decline bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering one of the dumbbells until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

6) Repeat with the other side.

name: Decline Bench Press.
description:
1. Sat at the end of a decline bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes or slightly behind. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

2. Your feet should be firmly planted and if possible, your legs should be secured with a pad

3. Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands and plant your feet into the ground

4. Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the middle of your chest, this is your starting position

5. Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

6. The bar should finish at the base of your sternum (breastbone), either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

7. Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

8. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

9. Ensure correct starting position and repeat

name: Decline Dumbbell Fly
description:
1) Set up on a decline bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you with the palms facing each other.

3) With the elbows slightly unlocked, begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells in an arc to the outside of the body.

4) Depending on mobility, the hands should finish in a position in line with the ears.

5) Proceed to then drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up in an arc and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Decline Dumbbell Press
description:
1. Set up on a decline bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand

2. Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle

3. Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells until the handles are level with the outside of the chest and the arms are at a 45-degree angle to the body

4. Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top

5. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core

name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension – Neutral
description:
1. Lie flat on a decline bench with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

6. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

7. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

8. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension – Pronating
description:
1. Lie flat on a decline bench with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a pronated position, palms away from you

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands as you lower the dumbbells, so you finish with a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

6. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

7. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

8. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner by reversing the movement, keeping constant tension in your triceps

9. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Decline EZ Bar Triceps Extension
description:
1. Lie flat on a bench with an EZ bar at shoulder-width apart

2. Plant your feet and lift the bar straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the bar in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

5. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

7. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Decline Push Up.
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Position a raised surface behind you such as a step or box (difficulty increases with height) and extend one leg back at a time, so they are positioned atop. Assume a push up position with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

3. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

4. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

5. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

6. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

7. Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

8. From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

name: Deficit Barbell Deadlift
description:
1. Stand on a plate(s) or a small platform

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Deficit Trap Bar Deadlift
description:
1. Stand on a plate(s) or a small platform

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. Stand in the middle of the trap bar, with the middle of your foot in line with the center of the handles (this helps with balance)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side
5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, lifting the bar off the floor. Keep the bar path in as straight a vertical line as possible)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar travel down in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Double Kettlebell Swing.
description:
1. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand, grabbing them at the top of the horns. The kettlebells should be in front of your thighs with your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by taking a big inhale and then hinging at the hips and pulling the kettlebells back between the legs, keeping the knees soft

5. The upper arms should be against the ribs and the forearms high in the groin

6. Once the hips are loaded and you have reached the end of your range of motion, reverse the motion by explosively driving your hips forward while pushing down into the ground with the feet

7. Exhale as you drive your hips forward and contract your glutes at the top

8. Bring the body to a standing position. Once the kettlebells are less than shoulder height, let them come back down on the same arc they went up

9. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Dowel Squat
description:
1. Position a dowel behind your back, grasping one hand at the back of your head and the other hand at the base of your lower back. The dowel should remain in contact with these two points throughout the entire movement

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up, keeping the dowel in contact at all times

9. Pause and repeat

name: Dowell Hip Hinge
description:
1. Stand with your feet hips-width apart and toes pointed forward

2. Position a dowel behind your back so one hand is holding it behind your head, while the other hand is at your lower back. The back of this hand will be used to hold the dowel in position, not the palm

3. The dowel should now be positioned so it has 3 points of contact with the body: the back of the head, between the shoulder blades, and top of the glutes

4. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum)

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by pushing the hips forward

6. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the core and glutes

7. If the knees start to bend or the dowel loses a point of contact with the body, then either you’ve gone past the mobility of your hips/hamstrings or the neutral spine position has been lost

name: Down Dog Terminal Knee Extension
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position on your hands and knees

2. Position a band around one knee and then hold down the ends with both hands

3. Ensuring you have a neutral spine, engage your core, and extend your legs back. Get into the down dog position by pushing your hips in and raising your heels slightly off the floor

4. Begin by bending the banded knee in a slow controlled manner and bringing it in towards the middle of the mat. As you move your knee your heel should come forward and you should finish with your toes pointed down

5. Reverse the movement by pushing your knee back against the band, squeezing your quad (front of thigh), and glute as you do so

name: Dumbbell 'I', 'Y', 'T'
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and your arms straight by your side. Grab a dumbbell with one hand

2. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

3. Let your arms hang by your side, using the free hand to brace against your leg

4. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down

5. Begin the movement by raising the dumbbell directly in front in a controlled manner, ensuring the arm stays straight

6. Pause and then lower the dumbbell under control to the start position, then repeat but this time raise the arm at a 45-degree angle

7. Pause and then lower the dumbbell under control to the start position, then repeat but this time raise the dumbbell straight out to the side so you form half a T' with the body

8. Return to the start position and then repeat

9. As you raise the dumbbell it's important that you don't shrug your shoulders up to your ears and that you keep your shoulder blades retracted. Also, avoid any momentum with your arm, ensuring you keep a neutral spine throughout

name: Dumbbell Alternating Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Dumbbell B-Stance RDL
description:
1. Grab a dumbbell in one hand

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Get into a b-stance position by taking a small step back with the leg on the opposite side to the dumbbell. Slightly bend at the knee with the back leg and go onto your toes. The front foot should be flat and knee soft

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. Make sure to keep the front knee soft and avoid locking it out

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the dumbbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Dumbbell Bent-Over Reverse Flies
description:
1. Grasp a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip and stand shoulder-width apart

2. Retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down

3. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows, your arms should be straight but with your elbows soft

4. Your back should be straight and your spine in a neutral position

5. Then raise the dumbbells to the side by driving your elbows back and raising the dumbbells. Your arms should be at or near a 90-degree angle to your body

6. Ensure your shoulders don't raise up towards your ears

7. Pause and squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

8. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders while maintaining a neutral spine

9. Be careful not to bend or straighten your arms as you raise the dumbbells up and down, ensuring the same angle throughout. Make sure you don't use any momentum or swinging to raise the dumbbells

name: Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Dumbbell Curl To Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder-width stance with your feet

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip. Position your palms facing the midline of the body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending your elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them up to the top of your shoulders and then from this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbells above the head

6. Make sure the dumbbells finish above the head at shoulder width, with the biceps in line with the ears

7. Ensure the shoulders don't elevate up towards the ears during the pressing movement

8. Proceed to lower the dumbbells back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, biceps, and glutes

name: Dumbbell Curtsy Lunge
description:
1) Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2) Start with feet hip-width apart and facing forward

3) Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back at a 45-degree angle with the leg which is the same side as the dumbbell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle. Keep on the toes with the back foot

4) Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

5) The front leg should also finish at a 90-degree angle, while the foot should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6) Keep the knee of the front leg stationary and pointed forward over your toes throughout
