7) From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot while maintaining an upright torso. Make sure to keep constant tension in the core throughout the movement

name: Dumbbell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Dumbbell External Rotation - Elbow On Knee
description:
1. Sit on the floor or a bench with one knee bent. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell

2. Position your elbow on your knee, resting it on the crease to the side of the knee. Your arm should be at a ninety-degree angle

3. Use your other hand to support and brace yourself against the floor/bench. Ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin the movement by lowering the dumbbell in a slow controlled manner, ensuring your elbow stays in contact with your knee and the arm stays at a ninety-degree angle

5. The movement is coming solely from the shoulder, so avoid any movement in the wrist. When you reach the end of your range, pause. and then return to the start position

name: Dumbbell Farmers Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in both hands

2. Dumbbells should be held by your sides but not touching

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Dumbbell French Press
description: Perform on an incline bench, about 45-60 degrees.

name: Dumbbell Front Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself in front of a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position. The front leg will be at less of an angle to the back leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Dumbbell Front Squat.
description:
1. To set up, begin with a curl or clean the dumbbells into the front rack position with the weights resting on your shoulders

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Dumbbell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a dumbbell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands

4. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

5. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

7. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Dumbbell Goblet Paused Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once the foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

7. Once sat, relax the leg and hip muscles and pause for one or two seconds. It’s important that you keep your spine stable by keeping a rigid torso

8. Stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Dumbbell Goblet Rocking Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once the foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

7. Once sat, rock back by bringing your torso into an upright position, then lean forward and stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. When you rock back its important that you don’t relax all your muscles, particularly your core

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Dumbbell Goblet Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

3. Once foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Dumbbell Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

2. Dumbbells should be held by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, then maintain this position for the allotted time while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Dumbbell Lateral Step Up
description:
1. Stand parallel to a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand with your arms by your side

3. Place the inside foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping down with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Dumbbell Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and pushing backward, maintaining an upright torso

name: Dumbbell Overhead Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in both hands

2. Lift the dumbbells above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulders, elbows, and wrists

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Dumbbell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand. Dumbbells should be in front of your thighs

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The dumbbells should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the dumbbells up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Dumbbell Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should either be facing laces down or have the toes pointed down into the step

4. Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Dumbbell Renegade Row.
description:
1. Get into a quadruped position on your hands and knees with two dumbbells

2. Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

3. Get into the start position by straitening both legs. Arms should be straight, eyes looking down and core braced

4. Begin by retracting your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the dumbbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Movement through the body should be limited to the moving arm, hips and spine staying as stationary as possible

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning the dumbbell to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Dumbbell Reverse Lunge.
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Dumbbell Single Prone T
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Grasp a dumbbell in one hand and position your arm straight out to the side with the dumbbell resting on the floor, the other hand braced against the floor or bench. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Get into the starting position by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then externally rotate the arm with the dumbbell so your palm is facing forward and your thumb is facing up

4. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the dumbbell in a controlled manner

5. Make sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

6. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Dumbbell Single Prone Y
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Grasp a dumbbell in one hand and position your arm at a 45-degree angle, 1/2 a 'Y', and with the dumbbell resting on the floor. The other hand braced against the floor or bench. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Get into the starting position by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then externally rotate the arm with the dumbbell so your thumb is facing up

4. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the dumbbell in a controlled manner

5. Make sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

6. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Dumbbell Split Squat.
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

3. Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Dumbbell Step Up.
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you dumbbell in each hand and have arms by your side

3. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Dumbbell Suitcase Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Dumbbell should be held by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t dip to the side at any point

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Dumbbell Suitcase Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Dumbbell should be help by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t dip to the side at any point

5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Dumbbell Sumo Goblet Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet wider than your normal squat stance and slightly turn them out. Mobility varies in people so find a position that’s comfortable for you

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

3. Once foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

5. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

6. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

7. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

8. Pause and repeat

name: Dumbbell Walking Lunges
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand with your arms by your side

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be at a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout this movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, step forward by driving through the floor with the front leg and stepping forward with the trail leg so you finish in a standing position with your feet side-by-side

9. Repeat going forward

name: Dumbbell Windmill
description:
1. Stand just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully lift up a dumbbell with one hand

2. Brace your core and lift the dumbbell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

3. Begin by bracing your core and hinging at your hips, keeping your knees soft. Your free arm should be straight and as you hinge your hips this hand moves towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

4. Your torso will slightly rotate as you lower but you should never lose a neutral spine

5. The arm with the dumbbell should be perpendicular to the floor throughout

6. Keep your eyes fixed on the dumbbell at all times

7. Stop when you reach the end of your range of motion and then reverse the movement, making sure to keep constant tension in your core while keeping your eyes fixed on the dumbbell

name: Dumbbell Zottman Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbells in, so your palms are facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps, forearms, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Dumbbells To Side RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand. Dumbbells should at the side of your thighs

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The dumbbells should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line. Your weight should be balanced over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the dumbbells up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Eccentric Push Up
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

3. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

4. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

5. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

6. Descend as slow as possible until you reach the floor, carefully dropping if needed.

name: Eccentric Push Up - Knees
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

3. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

4. Slightly push one knee back at a time with the arms straight and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

5. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

6. Descend as slow as possible until you reach the floor, carefully dropping if needed.

name: Eccentric Sliding Leg Curl - Single-Leg Back
description:
1. Set up on your back with your heels on sliding plates, your socks, or a towel. Your legs should be bent and your feet in towards your glutes.

2. Arms flat by your side. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis by tucking your tail bone-in and then brace your core. You should have a neutral spine for the entire movement

3. Start by extending your hips so they raise off the floor. Then begin the movement by sliding your feet forward and extending your legs in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep the hips up at all times

4. Ensure you keep constant tension in your glutes and hamstrings

5. When your legs are straightened out, pause and then slide one foot back to the start position, quickly followed by the other leg. Keep your hips extended up as you slide your feet back. Reset and repeat

name: Eccentric Sliding Leg Curl Isometric
description:
1. Set up on your back with your heels on sliding plates or a towel. Your legs should be bent and your feet in towards your glutes.

2. Arms flat by your side. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis by tucking your tail bone-in and then brace your core. You should have a neutral spine for the entire movement

3. Start by extending your hips so they raise off the floor. Then begin the movement by sliding your feet forward and extending your legs in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep the hips up at all times

4. Ensure you keep constant tension in your glutes and hamstrings

5. When your legs are almost straight, pause and hold this position for the allotted time, making sure to keep your hips extended off the floor while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Eccentric Standard Dip - Leg Assist
description:
1. Stand on a step with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart on a dip bar

2. Your legs should be straight with your feet slightly behind you on the step. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft

3. Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine and that your shoulders are depressed down

4. Start by lifting your feet from the step and holding your weight

5. Then begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, maintaining constant tension in your triceps

6. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

7. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

8. Pause at the bottom and place your feet back on the step. Then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, using your legs to assist you as much as needed. Contract your triceps when you reach the top

name: Eccentric to Knees Push Up
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

3. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

4. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

5. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

6. Descend as slow as possible until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

7. Carefully drop both knees to the floor while keeping your core braced in a neutral position.

8. From this position drive your hands through the floor and push back up, keeping constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

9. Repeat by getting back into start position.

name: Eccentric Towel Slides
description:
1. Set up on your back with your heels on sliding plates, your socks, or a towel. Your legs should be bent and your feet in towards your glutes.

2. Arms flat by your side. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis by tucking your tail bone-in and then brace your core. You should have a neutral spine for the entire movement

3. Start by extending your hips so they raise off the floor. Then begin the movement by sliding your feet forward and extending your legs in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep the hips up at all times

4. Ensure you keep constant tension in your glutes and hamstrings

5. When you reach the end, touch the floor and bring your feet back to the start position. Reset and repeat

name: Elbow and Knee Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched

2. Bend your knees so they’re in a 90-degree angle with your feet behind you

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line from your knee to shoulder, keeping your knee on the floor

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Elbow Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Elbow Side Plank with Band Hold
description:
1. Anchor a band and hold in one hand with your arm straight

2. Then lie on the side that has no band with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched. There should be no slack in the band

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes. The aim is to stop yourself rotating forward with the band

name: Elbow Side Plank with Dumbbell Overhead
description:
1. Lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Grab a dumbbell with your free hand

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line, then pull the dumbbell up so your arm is straight and perpendicular to the floor. Your shoulder, elbow and wrist should be in a straight line

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Erector Spinae Stretch
description:
1. Take a seat and place your feet together

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine and you have a nice upright posture

3. Place one hand on the seat and the other by your side

4. Start by carefully bending forward and to the side, reaching your hand towards the outside of the opposite foot.

5. As you reach forward be sure not to excessively bend your back

6. You should feel a stretch in your lower/middle back

7. Hold for allotted time and then carefully return to the start position

name: EZ Bar California Press
description:
1. Lie flat on a bench with an EZ bar at shoulder-width apart

2. Plant your feet and lift the bar straight above your head so its directly above your throat. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

4. Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the bar in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows forward and bringing the bar towards your throat

5. Bring the bar as low as your ability allows you and then return to the start position, keeping constant tension in your triceps

6. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: EZ Bar Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you an EZ bar and grab shoulder-width apart

3. Place your arms straight by your side and brace your core. Make sure you have a neutral spine, and your arms aren’t locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the bar up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the bar, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: EZ Bar Reverse Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you an EZ bar and grab with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart

3. Place your arms straight by your side and brace your core. Make sure you have a neutral spine, and your arms aren’t locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the bar up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and forearms, and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the bar, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps, forearms and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Feet Banded Anterior Tibialis Raise
description:
1. Anchor a band and take a seated position on the floor with your legs straight. Hook the band around the front of both feet, which should be close together

2. Sit far enough away so there’s no slack in the band

3. Place your hands behind you for support and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin the movement by lowering your toes in a slow controlled manner. When you can’t go any further, pause and pull your toes in towards you as much as possible. Keep your heels down as you pull

5. Contract your shin muscles and then repeat

name: Feet Elevated Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lie on your back in front of a box or bench

2. Place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up. Position yourself closer or further from the box so your knees will be at a 90-degree angle when you’re at the top of the movement

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your feet or your heels

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Feet Elevated Glute Bridge w Band
description:
1. Lie on your back in front of a box or bench and position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

2. Place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up. Position yourself closer or further from the box so your knees will be at a 90-degree angle when you’re at the top of the movement

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your feet or your heels. Drive knees out against the band

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, pushing the knees out against the band and keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Feet-Elevated Hip Thrust
description:
1. Assume your hip thrust position and place a box securely in front of you where your toes normally would be for a regular hip thrust. You can place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up

2. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

3. Position the box closer or further away so your knees will be slightly greater than a 90-degree angle when you’re at the bottom of the movement

4. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your feet of your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core

name: Feet-Elevated Hip Thrust with Mini Band
description:
1. Assume your hip thrust position and place a box securely in front of you where your toes normally would be for a regular hip thrust. You can place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

4. Position the box closer or further away so your knees will be slightly greater than a 90-degree angle when you’re at the bottom of the movement

5. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out against the band and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your feet of your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core

name: Fire Hydrant.
description:
1. Get into a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

2. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, making sure the spine is in a neutral position

3. Begin the movement by rotating one of your legs out to the side at a 90-degree angle under control

4. As you move it's important that the movement only comes from externally rotating your hip upwards

5. Don't rock or lean to the opposite side when you raise your leg as doing so will result in you using momentum to raise your leg and take away from the target muscle working correctly

6. Make sure your torso stays stationary and your spine neutral throughout

7. Go as high as your range of motion allows you to, then pause slightly and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Flat Dumbbell Fly
description:
1) Set up on a flat bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you with the palms facing each other.

3) With the elbows slightly unlocked, begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells in an arc to the outside of the body.

4) Depending on mobility, the hands should finish in a position in line with the ears.

5) Proceed to then drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up in an arc and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Flat Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set up on a flat bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells until the handles are level with the outside of the chest and the arms are at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Flat Dumbbell Triceps Extension – Neutral
description:
1. Lie back on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

6. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

7. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

8. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Flat Dumbbell Triceps Extension – Pronating
description:
1. Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a pronated position, palms away from you

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands as you lower the dumbbells, so you finish with a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

6. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

7. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

8. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner by reversing the movement, keeping constant tension in your triceps

9. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Flat EZ Bar Accentuated Eccentric Press
description:
1. Lie flat on a bench with an EZ bar at shoulder-width apart

2. Plant your feet and lift the bar straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the bar in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

5. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and bring the bar forward to your chest, keeping the bar as close to your body as you can. From here, press the bar up to the starting position

7. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Flat EZ Bar Triceps Extension
description:
1. Lie flat on a bench with an EZ bar at shoulder-width apart

2. Plant your feet and lift the bar straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the bar in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

5. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

7. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Floor Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Take up a seated position on the floor and pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells with the arms are at a 45-degree angle to the body until your elbows touch the floor.

4) Control the dumbbells down and be careful not to bang your elbows off the floor.

5) While keeping your back flat to the floor, proceed to push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Floor Dumbbell Press (Bridge Position)
description:
1) Take up a seated position on the floor and pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Then perform a glute bridge and hold your. hips in the air. Keep them raised for the entirety of the set, unless you start to fatigue and your technique will be hindered.

4) From this position begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells with the arms at a 45-degree angle to the body until your elbows touch the floor.

5) Control the dumbbells down and be careful not to bang your elbows off the floor.

6) While keeping your back flat on the floor, proceed to push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

7) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Floor Seated Face Pull w External Rotation
description:
1. Take up a seated position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height

2. With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip

3. Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart and bring it towards the top of your head. Hands should finish to the side of your head with your arms near a 90-degree angle. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Floor Seated Rope Face Pull
description:
1. Sit on the floor in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip. Brace yourself

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Foam Roller Body Saw
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Then place your forearms on to a foam roller and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Begin by pushing the foam roller forward with your forearms in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep your hips up and your core engaged

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then bring the roller back to the start position

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Foot Banded Anterior Tibialis Raise
description:
1. Anchor a band and take a seated position on the floor with one leg straight and the other bent. Hook the band around the front of the foot on the straight leg

2. Sit far enough away so there’s no slack in the band and place the other foot flat

3. Place your hands behind you for support and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin the movement by lowering your toes in a slow controlled manner. When you can’t go any further, pause and pull your toes in towards you as much as possible. Keep your heel down as you pull

5. Contract your shin muscle and then repeat

name: Foot Banded Lateral Walk
description:
1. Place a band around your feet and place your feet wide enough apart to create tension in the band

2. With a neutral spine, hinge at the hips slightly by pushing your bum back, your knees should be in line with your toes and slightly bent

3. Interlock your hands out in front to help with balance

4. From here, while bracing your core, take a step to the side by stepping with the foot

5. Your foot shouldn't come too high off the floor when you step. Land with your foot flat, keeping your weight centered over the middle of your foot

6. As you step, your foot and knee should stay aligned, so as you move your knee is stacked directly on top of your foot

7. The other leg should stationary, making sure to keep the knee aligned over the foot and resist and inward movement. This leg provides resistance by stretching the band

8. Once you've planted your foot, step across with the stationary leg so you end up in the starting position

9. Step across for desired reps and then repeat going the opposite direction

name: Foot Elevated Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lie on your back in front of a box or bench

2. Place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up. Position yourself closer or further from the box so your knee will be at a 90-degree angle when you’re at the top of the movement. Feet should be together along your centre line

3. Elevate one leg. The leg can either be bent or straight, choose what works best for you

4. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

5. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your foot or your heel

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Foot Elevated Glute Bridge w Band
description:
1. Lie on your back in front of a box or bench and position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

2. Place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up. Position yourself closer or further from the box so your knee will be at a 90-degree angle when you’re at the top of the movement

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your foot or your heel

6. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Foot-Elevated Hip Thrust
description:
1. Assume your hip thrust position and place a box securely in front of you where your toes normally would be for a regular hip thrust. You can place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up

2. Position the box closer or further away so your knee will be slightly greater than a 90-degree angle when you’re at the bottom of the movement

3. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

4. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

5. Bring your feet together along your centre line and lift one leg up in the air. You can keep this leg bent or straight, whatever works best for you

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your foot or your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Foot-Elevated Hip Thrust with Mini Band
description:
1. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

2. Assume your hip thrust position and place a box securely in front of you where your toes normally would be for a regular hip thrust. You can place the middle of your feet on the edge of the box or place both heels on the top with your toes pointed up

3. Position the box closer or further away so your knee will be slightly greater than a 90-degree angle when you’re at the bottom of the movement

4. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Bring your feet together along your centre line and lift one leg up in the air. You can keep this leg bent or straight, whatever works best for you

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through the centre of your foot or your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

8. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

9. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Forward Leaning OH Rope Triceps's Extensions
description:
1. With handles above your head, lean upper body forward while maintaining a straight back.

2. Elbows should be tucked in beside head, pointing forward.

3. Drive the handles out in front and extend your arms, squeezing your triceps at the end of the movement.

4. Return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping tension in the triceps and maintaining upper body position.

5. The angle of your upper arm and the cable should run parallel to each other during the movement. This ensures the path of tension is at it's highest.

name: Forward Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

3. Keep on the toes with the back foot

4. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

5. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

6. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

7. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Frog Pump
description:
1. Lie on your back with your heels together

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

4. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the outside of your heels

6. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Frog Pump w Mini Band
description:
1. Lie on your back with your heels together

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the outside of your heels

7. Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout, making sure to push out against the band at all times

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Front Foot Elevated Single DB Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself in front of a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position. The front leg will be at less of an angle to the back leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. Pick up a dumbbell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Front Foot Elevated Single KB Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself in front of a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position. The front leg will be at less of an angle to the back leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. Pick up a kettlebell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Front Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself in front of a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position. The front leg will be at less of an angle to the back leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Full Kneeling Foam Roller Ankle Plantar Flexion
Full Kneeling Foam Roller Quad Stretch
Full Kneeling Kettlebell Halos
description:
1. Assume a full kneeling position on the floor with both knees down. Have a mat or pad under you

2. Grab a kettlebell by the horns with both hands and hold it in front of the body

3. Brace your core and carefully bring the kettlebell to eye level, with the bottom of the kettlebell facing up

4. With a braced core and a neutral spine, slowly rotate the kettlebell around your head in a circular fashion making sure to keep your torso stable throughout

5. Ensure your head doesn’t protrude forward and that your spine stays neutral

6. Keep the kettlebell close to your head when rotating and finish when you reach the start position. Repeat going the opposite direction

name: Full-Kneeling Pallof Press
description:
1) Assume a full kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height

2) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5) Hands should go out in a straight line

6) Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

7) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

name: Full-Kneeling Pallof Press Hold
description:
1) Assume a full kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height

2) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5) Pause in this position and hold for the allotted time
6) Your core should be engaged with as minimal movement in the body as possible for the entire duration of the set

7) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

8) When time finishes, return the handle or band in a slow controlled manner

name: Full-Kneeling Rope Chop
description:
1) Assume a full kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine with a rope attached at the top

2) Grab the rope with both hands in a shoulder-width position so the arms are extended up and in front of the body

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, chop the rope down in front of the body

5) There should be no movement in your torso during the chop, only in the arms chopping the rope down

6) The rope should finish beside the outside hip

7) Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

8) The spine should stay in a neutral position during the entire movement

9) The rotation should be a crossbody pattern, think about moving diagonally rather than up and down

name: Full-Kneeling Rope Lift
description:
1. Assume a full kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine with a rope attached at the bottom

2. Grab the rope with both hands in a shoulder-width position so the arms are extended in front and to the side of the body

3. Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4. From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, lift the rope in a crossbody pattern. Think about moving diagonally rather than up and down

5. There should be no movement in your torso during the lift, only in the arms lifting the rope up

6. When end range of motion is reached pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

7. The spine should stay in a neutral position during the entire movement

name: Glute Bridge w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

6. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

8. Drive the knees out against the band. Knees should track your middle toes throughout, and your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

9. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, continuing to push your knees out against the band and keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Glute Bridge.
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

4. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

5. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

7. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Half-Kneeling 45* Single Arm Pulldown
description:
1) Position a handle attachment at the top of a cable machine and grab with one hand.

2) Take up a half-kneeling position, with the handle the same side as the downed knee.

3) Arm should be in a 45-degree angle when the start position is set. Depending on an individual’s mobility around the shoulder, you can decrease or increase the angle to match ability.

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, begin the movement by depressing the shoulder down and retracting the shoulder blade back.

5) Then pull the handle in towards the body by bringing the elbow down towards the ribs. In this position squeeze your lats and contract.

6) Ensure the shoulder never elevates up towards the ear during the movement.

7) Proceed to return the handle back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, core and glutes.

name: Half-Kneeling Cable Row
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

3. Attach a handle so it’s just below chest height and grab it with the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee. Palm facing in

4. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

5. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulder depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Half-Kneeling Face Pull w External Rotation
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height

2. With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip

3. Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back
