5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart and bring it towards the top of your head. Hands should finish to the side of your head with your arms near a 90-degree angle. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Half-Kneeling High Rope Face Pull
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope to the top

2. With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip

3. Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows down and back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart so it finishes to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Half-Kneeling Kettlebell Halos
description:
1. Assume a half-kneeling position on the floor with one knee down and one foot flat. Have a mat or pad under the downed knee. Knees should be at 90-degree angles

2. Grab a kettlebell by the horns with both hands and hold it in front of the body

3. Brace your core and carefully bring the kettlebell to eye level, with the bottom of the kettlebell facing up

4. With a braced core and a neutral spine, slowly rotate the kettlebell around your head in a circular fashion making sure to keep your torso stable throughout

5. Ensure your head doesn’t protrude forward and that your spine stays neutral

6. Keep the kettlebell close to your head when rotating and finish when you reach the start position

7. Repeat going the same direction and then swap legs

name: Half-kneeling Kettlebell Press
description:
1) Take up a half-kneeling position.

2) Pick up a kettlebell with the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee.

3) Position the kettlebell in a rack position at the front of the shoulder. The kettlebell should be resting on the outside of the forearm, elbow pointing down, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body.

4) Make sure to engage your core and glutes.

5) From this position, drive the kettlebell straight up while at the same time externally rotating the arm. Your palm should finish facing forward at the top position.

6) Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

7) Proceed to lower the kettlebell back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes.

name: Half-Kneeling Landmine Press
description:
1) Facing the landmine take up a half-kneeling position.

2) Carefully lift the bar up or have a partner hand it to you. Grab the bar (with the hand that is the same side as the downed knee) at the top of the barbell and position at the top of the shoulder with the palm facing away from you.

3) From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the bar up.

4) Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

5) Proceed to lower the bar back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes.

name: Half-Kneeling Landmine Y Press
description:
1) While parallel to the landmine, take up a half-kneeling position with the knee closest to the barbell on the floor.

2) Carefully lift the bar up or have a partner hand it to you. Grab the bar at the top of the barbell and position at the top of the shoulder with the palm facing away from you.

3) From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the bar up in a “Y” pattern.

4) Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

5) Proceed to lower the bar back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes.

name: Half-Kneeling Neutral Grip Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Take up a half-kneeling position.

2) Grab a dumbbell with the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee.

3) Position the dumbbell at the top of the shoulder with the palm of your hand facing inwards toward the midline of the body.

4). From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbell straight up.

5). Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

6) Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes.

name: Half-Kneeling Neutral Grip High Face Pull
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach two handles at the top of the machine

2. With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab a handle with each hand with a neutral grip

3. Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the handles in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows down and back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the handles apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spin

name: Half-Kneeling Pallof Press
description:
1) Assume a half-kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height. Knee closest should be on the floor

2) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5) Hands should go out in a straight line

6) Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

7) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

name: Half-kneeling Pallof Press Hold
description:
1) Assume a half-kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height. Knee closest should be on the floor

2) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5) Pause in this position and hold for the allotted time

6) Your core should be engaged with as minimal movement in the body as possible for the entire duration of the set

7) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

8) When time finishes, return the handle or band in a slow controlled manner

name: Half-Kneeling Rope Chop
description:
1. Assume a half-kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine with a rope attached at the top. Knee furthest from the machine should be on the floor

2. Grab the rope with both hands in a shoulder-width position so the arms are extended up and in front of the body

3. Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4. From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, chop the rope down in front of the body towards the downed knee

5. There should be no movement in your torso during the chop, only in the arms chopping the rope down

6. The rope should finish beside the downed knee

7. Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

8. The spine should stay in a neutral position during the entire movement

9. The rotation should be a crossbody pattern, think about moving diagonally rather than up and down

name: Half-Kneeling Rope Face Pull
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height

2. With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip

3. Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart so it finishes to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Half-Kneeling Rope Lift
description:
1. Assume a half kneeling position on the floor, parallel to a cable machine with a rope attached at the bottom. Knee closest to the machine should be on the floor

2. Grab the rope with both hands in a shoulder-width position so the arms are extended and in front of the downed knee

3. Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4. From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, lift the rope in a crossbody pattern. Think about moving diagonally rather than up and down

5. There should be no movement in your torso during the lift, only in the arms lifting the rope up

6. When end range of motion is reached pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

7. The spine should stay in a neutral position during the entire movement

name: Half-Kneeling Rope Single Arm Row
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s just below chest height

2. With an upright posture, grab the rope with a neutral grip in the hand that’s opposite to the downed knee and brace yourself

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the rope in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Half-Kneeling Rotation With Dowel
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

3. Place a dowel on your upper back and grasp slightly wider than shoulder-width apart

4. Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

5. Begin by slowly rotating the dowel to one side as far as you can, concentrating on staying as tall as possible creating a straight line from the ear, shoulder, hip, and down knee for proper posture alignment

6. At the end of your range, pause slightly and take a deep breath, then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

7. Repeat on the other side

name: Half-Kneeling Rotation with Forward Lean
description:
1. Take up a half kneeling position on the floor and place both hands flat on the floor either side of the front foot

2. While keeping the hand that's opposite the downed foot flat the floor, carefully rotate the other arm straight up

3. As you rotate your head should follow your hand as it moves

4. Pause when you reach the end of your range of motion and then return to the start position

5. Keep your hips and lower body as stationary as stationary as you can as you move

6. Exhale as you rotate up

name: Half-Kneeling Single Pronated Row
description:
1. Take up a half-kneeling position in front of a cable machine and attach a handle so it’s just below chest height

2. With an upright posture, grab the handle with an overhand grip in the hand that’s opposite to the downed knee and brace yourself

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner in line with the top of the chest by rowing your elbow back and out to your side

5. Arm should finish almost parallel to the floor and in a 90-degree angle. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your middle back, rather than just your arm

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Hanging Knee Raise With Ball Between Knees
description:
1. Squeeze a medicine ball or slam ball between your feet.

2. Then hang from a pull up bar with the ball between your feet.

3. Keeping your core in a braced, neutral position, lift your knees towards your chest.

4. Pause slightly at the top and lower legs under control.

5. Keep squeezing the ball throughout.

name: Hanging Knee Raises
description:
1. Hang from a pull up bar with knees bent.

2. Keeping your core in a braced, neutral position, lift your knees towards your chest.

3. Pause slightly at the top and lower legs under control.

name: Heel Elevated Kettlebell Rack Squat
description:
1. Elevate your heels on a wedge or 2 plates

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Then clean them up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

4. Once the foot position is set and the kettlebells are in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Heels Elevated Dumbbell Goblet Squat
description:
1. Elevate your heels on a wedge or 2 plates

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height either by the bell underneath or the horns on the handle. The dumbbell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Heels Elevated Kettlebell Goblet Squat
description:
1. Elevate your heels on a wedge or 2 plates

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell. Hold it at chest height either by the bell underneath or the horns on the handle. The kettlebell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once foot position is set and the kettlebell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Heels Elevated Narrow Stance DB Squat
Heels Elevated Squat
description:
1. Elevate your heels on a wedge or 2 plates

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

7. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

8. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Hip Hinge Single-Leg With Dowel
description:
1. Position a dowel behind your back, grasping one hand at the back of your head and the other hand at the base of your lower back. The dowel should remain in contact with these two points throughout the entire movement

2. Place your feet hip-width apart. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes.

3. From here, while bracing your core, begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting one leg off the floor

4. Your torso and back leg should move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

5. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when your torso is moving towards the floor

6. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

7. Lower your torso in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion, keeping the dowel in contact at all times

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

9. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

3. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core

name: Hip Thrust w Mini Band
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

4. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out against the band and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core

name: Hip-Banded Hip Thrust
description:
1. Attach a band on a rack or around crisscrossed heavy dumbbells and position over your hips. There should be little to no slack in the band when you’re sat

2. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

4. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up through the band by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core

name: Hip-Banded Single-Leg Hip Thrust
description:
1. Attach a band on a rack or around crisscrossed heavy dumbbells and position over your hips. There should be little to no slack in the band when you’re sat

2. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

4. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet along your centre line and elevate one leg up. You can keep your leg straight or bent, whatever you prefer

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by through the band by pushing through your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Incline Bench Press.
description:
1. Set up an incline bench. Typical angle is 30-degrees but can vary between 15-45 degrees depending on personal preference

2. Sat at the end of a bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes or slightly behind. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

3. Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands and plant your feet into the ground

4. Next, pinch your shoulder blades together and push your chest out. You should then have a slight arch in your lower back with your upper back pressed against the bench

5. Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the top of your chest, this is your starting position

6. Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

7. The bar should finish towards the top of your chest, either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

8. Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

9. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

10. Ensure correct starting position and repeat

name: Incline Bench Wide Grip BB Shrugs
Incline Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. Begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed in their starting position

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Incline Dumbbell Fly
description:
1) Set a bench up to an incline, typically between 15-30 degrees and then carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you with the palms facing each other.

3) With the elbows slightly unlocked, begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells in an arc to the outside of the body.

4) Depending on mobility, the hands should finish in a position in line with the ears.

5) Proceed to then drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up in an arc and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Incline Dumbbell Hammer Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

5. Begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed in their starting position

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set a bench up to an incline, typically between 15-30 degrees and then carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbells until the handles are level with the outside of the chest and the arms are at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbells back up to the starting position by pushing them up and bringing the arms towards each other, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Incline Dumbbell Triceps Extension - Neutral
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 30–45-degree angle and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

6. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

7. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

8. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Incline Dumbbell Triceps Extension - Pronating
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 30–45-degree angle and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Position the dumbbells in a pronated position, palms away from you

4. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

5. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands as you lower the dumbbells, so you finish with a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

6. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

7. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

8. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner by reversing the movement, keeping constant tension in your triceps

9. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Incline Dumbbell Zottman Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. Begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed in their starting position

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbells in, so your palms are facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps, forearms, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Incline EZ Bar Triceps Extension
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 30–45-degree angle and sit back with an EZ bar at shoulder-width apart

2. Plant your feet and lift the bar straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

3. Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the bar in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

5. Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

7. Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

name: Incline Push Up.
description:
1. Place your hands under your shoulders on an elevated surface like a box or step (the higher the elevation, the easier the difficulty is), with your legs straight and toes pointed down into the floor

2. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

3. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

4. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

5. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

6. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

7. Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the step.

8. From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

name: Incline Supinating Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench between 45-75-degrees. The greater your mobility and strength, the less of an angle you can use to find what works for you

2. Sit back on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Place your arms straight by your side and pull them back so they’re perpendicular to the floor

3. Position the dumbbells in a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

5. Begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

6. As you bring them up, externally rotate the dumbbells so you finish with your palms facing up

7. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed in their starting position

8. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

9. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner by reversing the movement, ensuring constant tension on your biceps

10. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Inverted TRX Row
description:
1. Face a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Straighten your arms so the slack is taken off the straps

2. Palms facing each other in a neutral grip with eyes looking forward

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with an upright posture. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement

4. Walk-in with both feet while keeping the arms straight until your body is as parallel to the floor as possible. Equally, you can start from the floor. On your heels

5. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades. Quickly pull yourself up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Shoulders should not elevate up towards the ears at any point

8. Pause and then lower yourself to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back

name: Ipsilateral Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your arms straight in the air

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the arm on the same side to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

7. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Ipsilateral Dumbbell Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

3. Pick up a dumbbell in the hand that’s the opposite side as the downed knee and position on the outside of the leg

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Ipsilateral Kettlebell Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

3. Pick up a kettlebell in the hand that’s the opposite side as the downed knee and position on the outside of the leg

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Ipsilateral Knee Bent Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your arms straight in the air

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lower one foot towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the arm on the same side to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

7. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Ipsilateral Knee Bent Iso Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your hands flat against your knees

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lower one foot towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the arm on the same side to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. At the same time, use your other hand to press against your knee. Use your core to resist any movement and stop your leg from moving

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Ipsilateral Knee Bent Iso Dead Bug - Alternating
Isometric Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your hands flat against your knees

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the arm on the same side to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. At the same time, use your other hand to press against your knee. Use your core to resist any movement and stop your leg from moving

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Isometric Prone Back Extension
description:
1. Get into a prone position on the floor, lying on your stomach with your legs straight and feet together

2. Your head should be facing the floor and your hands by the side of your head. Ensure you have a neutral spine and your shoulder blades are pulled back

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your chest from the floor in a controlled manner, going as high as you can while maintaining the correct form

4. As you raise up ensure you avoid any movement in your legs and upper back

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor throughout the movement

6. When you reach the top, pause and hold this position for the allotted time, ensuring constant tension in your lower back and glutes

name: Kettlebell Alternating Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you kettlebell bell in each hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Kettlebell Behind Hip Hinge
description:
1. Stand with your feet hips-width apart and toes pointed forward

2. With both hands, position a kettlebell behind your back so it rests on the top of your glutes

3. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum)

4. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by pushing the hips forward

5. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the core and glutes

6. If the knees start to bend or your spine rounds, then either you’ve gone past the mobility of your hips/hamstrings or the neutral spine position has been lost

name: Kettlebell Curtsy Lunge
description:
1) Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2) Start with feet hip-width apart and facing forward

3) Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back at a 45-degree angle with the leg which is the same side as the dumbbell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle. Keep on the toes with the back foot

4) Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

5) The front leg should also finish at a 90-degree angle, while the foot should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6) Keep the knee of the front leg stationary and pointed forward over your toes throughout

7) From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot while maintaining an upright torso. Make sure to keep constant tension in the core throughout the movement

name: Kettlebell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Kettlebell Farmers Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in both hands

2. Kettlebells should be held by your sides but not touching

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Kettlebell Front Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself in front of a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position. The front leg will be at less of an angle to the back leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. Pick up a kettlebell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Kettlebell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands

4. Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

5. Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

7. Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

8. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

name: Kettlebell Goblet Paused Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once the foot position is set and the kettlebell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

7. Once sat, relax the leg and hip muscles and pause for one or two seconds. It’s important that you keep your spine stable by keeping a rigid torso

8. Stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Kettlebell Goblet Rocking Box Squat
description:
1. Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

2. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept close and tight to the body for the entire movement

4. Once the foot position is set and the kettlebell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

5. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

6. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

7. Once sat, rock back by bringing your torso into an upright position, then lean forward and stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. When you rock back its important that you don’t relax all your muscles, particularly your core

9. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

10. Pause and repeat

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat.
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell. Hold it at chest height either by the bell underneath or the horns on the handle. The kettlebell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

3. Once foot position is set and the kettlebell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Kettlebell Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand

2. Kettlebells should be held by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, then maintain this position for the allotted time while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Kettlebell Lateral Step Up
description:
1. Stand parallel to a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a Kettlebell in each hand with your arms by your side

3. Place the inside foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping down with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Kettlebell Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you kettlebell bell in each hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Kettlebell Overhead Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in both hands

2. Lift the kettlebells above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulders, elbows, and wrists

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Kettlebell Rack Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in both hands

2. Clean the kettlebells into the front rack position, locking your arms in tight to your torso

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Kettlebell Rack Marches
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in both hands

2. Clean the kettlebells into the front rack position, locking your arms in tight to your torso

3. Feet should be hip-width apart and pointed forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

5. Then raise one knee, bringing it towards your chest. Elevate the knee to a position where you can maintain a neutral spine, if you go too high you will lose this position with your spine

6. Hips should stay parallel with the floor when the leg is moving, while standing leg should be stationary

7. Pause at the top and then return the foot to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Kettlebell Rack Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Then clean them up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

3. Once the foot position is set and the kettlebells are in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Kettlebell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Kettlebells should be in front of your thighs

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the kettlebells in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The kettlebells should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the kettlebells up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Kettlebell Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should either be facing laces down or have the toes pointed down into the step

4. Pick up a kettlebell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Kettlebell Renegade Row
description:
1. Get into a quadruped position on your hands and knees with two kettlebells

2. Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

3. Get into the start position by straitening both legs. Arms should be straight, eyes looking down and core braced

4. Begin by retracting your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the kettlebell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Movement through the body should be limited to the moving arm, hips and spine staying as stationary as possible

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning the kettlebell to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Kettlebell Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

3. Pick up a kettlebell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Kettlebell Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you kettlebell in each hand and have arms by your side

3. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Kettlebell Suitcase Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Kettlebell should be held by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t dip to the side at any point

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Kettlebell Suitcase Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a Kettlebell in one hand

2. Kettlebell should be help by your side but not touching

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t dip to the side at any point

5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Kettlebell Sumo Goblet Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet wider than your normal squat stance and slightly turn them out. Mobility varies in people so find a position that’s comfortable for you

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell. Hold it at chest height either by the bell underneath or the horns on the handle. The kettlebell should be kept in close and tight to the body for the entire movement

3. Once foot position is set and the kettlebell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

5. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

6. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

7. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

8. Pause and repeat

name: Kettlebell Swing.
description:
1. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and grab at the top of the horns with both hands. The kettlebell should be in front of your thighs with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by taking a big inhale and then hinging at the hips and pulling the kettlebell back between the legs, keeping the knees soft

5. The upper arms should be against the ribs and the forearms high in the groin

6. Once the hips are loaded and you have reached the end of your range of motion, reverse the motion by explosively driving your hips forward while pushing down into the ground with the feet

7. Exhale as you drive your hips forward and contract your glutes at the top

8. Bring the body to a standing position. Once the kettlebell is less than shoulder height, let it come back down on the same arc it went up

9. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Kettlebell To Side RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Kettlebells should at the side of your thighs

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the kettlebells in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The kettlebells should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line. Your weight should be balanced over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the kettlebells up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Kettlebell Walking Lunges
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand with your arms by your side

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout this movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, step forward by driving through the floor with the front leg and stepping in with the trail leg so you finish in a standing position

name: Kettlebell Windmill.
description:
1. Stand just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully lift up a kettlebell with one hand

2. Brace your core and lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist with the kettlebell resting on the back of your wrist

3. Begin by bracing your core and hinging at your hips, keeping your knees soft. Your free arm should be straight and as you hinge your hips this hand moves towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

4. Your torso will slightly rotate as you lower but you should never lose a neutral spine

5. The arm with the kettlebell should be perpendicular to the floor throughout

6. Keep your eyes fixed on the kettlebell at all times

7. Stop when you reach the end of your range of motion and then reverse the movement, making sure to keep constant tension in your core while keeping your eyes fixed on the kettlebell

name: Knee Bent Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles and your arms straight in the air

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then lower one foot towards the floor while simultaneously lowering the opposite arm to the floor behind you in a slow controlled manner

4. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

5. Gently touch the floor with your heel and the back of your hand. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

7. Repeat on the opposite sides

name: Knee Scissor Stack and Smash - Lacrosse
Kneeling Banded Hip Extension
description:
1. Anchor a band at hip height to a secure surface such as a squat rack. Get into a full kneeling position and place the band around the front of your hips

2. Start in an upright position with a neutral spine and with your head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum)

4. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward against the band and contracting your glutes. Maintain constant tension through the core and glutes

name: Kneeling Landmine Meadows Row
description:
1. Attach a barbell to a landmine and position a bench side on at the top of the bar

2. Assume a kneeling position on the bench with your same side hand braced against the bench. The foot on the floor should be in a stable position

3. Carefully lift up the bar or have a partner hand it to you in your right hand with an overhand grip. Make sure you have a neutral spine with your head facing forward and your core is braced

4. Then retract your shoulder blade while also making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulders blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Knees Bent Inverted TRX Row
description:
1. Face a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Straighten your arms so the slack is taken off the straps

2. Palms facing each other in a neutral grip with eyes looking forward

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with an upright posture. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement

4. Walk-in with both feet while keeping the arms straight until your body is as parallel to the floor as possible. Equally, you can start from the floor

5. Bend your knees and have your feet flat

6. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades. Quickly pull yourself up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

7. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

8. Shoulders should not elevate up towards the ears at any point
