9. Pause and then lower yourself to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back

name: Knees Shoulder Taps
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then slide your knees back one at a time, increasing the distance between your hands and knees. Make sure to keep your core engaged

3. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor

4. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

5. Then lift one hand off the floor and touch the opposite shoulder. Pause and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

6. Aim is to resist any rotation in your body when you lift your hand. Keep constant tension in your core and glutes

7. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips up

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Landmine B-Stance Hip Thrust
description:
1. Position a bench to the side of a landmine

2. Secure the bench so it won’t slide backward. Sit in the front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Next, while seated, roll the top of the barbell over your legs and place it over your hip closest to the landmine. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Hold the bar in position with your hand

4. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Then straighten one leg, placing your heel just in front of the other foot, and point your toes in the air

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If the chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

7. The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Landmine Meadows Row
description:
1. Attach a barbell to a landmine and stand at the top of the bar facing towards it. Your feet should be shoulder width apart and your right foot should be slightly back

2. Carefully lift up the bar or have a partner hand it to you in your right hand with an overhand grip. Make sure you have a neutral spine with your head facing forward

3. Start by bracing your core and then hinge at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

4. Then retract your shoulder blade while also making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulders blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Landmine RDL
description:
1) While facing the landmine, position your feet about hip-width apart with toes facing forwards.

2) Carefully lift the bar up or have a partner hand it to you. While standing tall, grab the end of the barbell with both hands. Either clasp your hands together or have one behind the other.

3) Keep the arms straight so the bar is positioned just in front of the legs.

4) From here, while bracing your core and engaging your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits), begin by hinging at the hips.

5) Lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion.

6) Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them.

7) From the bottom position, drive the hips forward and bring the bar back up to the starting position while maintaining constant tension through the core, glutes and upper back.

name: Landmine Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad.

3. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you the end of the barbell attached to the landmine, grabbing it with the hand that is the same side as the downed knee

4. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Landmine Reverse Lunge
description:
1) Facing a landmine, carefully pick it up or have a partner hand it to you.

2) Start with feet hip-width apart and facing forward, with the landmine in one of your hands by your side.

3) Start by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg that's the same side as the hand holding the landmine. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle. Keep on the toes with the back foot.

4) Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force.

5) The front leg should also finish in a 90-degree angle, with the foot kept stationary and flat throughout the movement.

6) From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot while maintaining an upright torso, making sure you keep tension in the core throughout the entire movement.

name: Landmine Rotations
description:
1. Stand shoulder-width apart, facing a barbell attached to a landmine

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you the end of the barbell and grasp with both hands, overlapping if able

3. Your arms should be straight, and your hands should be at the side of one leg

4. Start by bracing your core and pulling the barbell across your body to the opposite side, ensuring your arms stay straight and you maintain a neutral spine

5. As you pull the bar across, pivot on the foot that’s the same side as the barbell's starting position. The opposite foot should stay planted

6. Your torso is going to rotate with the barbell, but your spine shouldn’t twist. As you pivot, your hips will rotate in the same direction as the barbell

7. Lower the barbell to the opposite side in a slow controlled manner, finishing beside the leg

8. Repeat in the other direction

name: Landmine Single Arm Row
description:
1. Position a landmine behind you and stand to one side of the barbell with your feet shoulder-width apart

2. Carefully lift the barbell up or have a partner hand it to you and grab with a neutral grip in one hand

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Landmine Single-Leg Hip Thrust
description:
1. Position a bench to the side of a landmine

2. Secure a bench step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in the front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Next, while seated, roll a barbell over your legs and place it over the hip closest to the landmine. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Hold the bar in position

4. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet along your center line and elevate one leg up. You can keep your leg straight or bent, whatever you prefer

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If the chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Landmine Single-Leg RDL
description:
1. Facing a landmine, start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you the landmine in one hand. The barbell should be positioned in front of one leg

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the landmine off the floor. At the same time lower the landmine in a controlled manner

6. Your torso, the landmine, and your back leg should all move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

7. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when the landmine is moving towards the floor

8. The standing leg should remain stationery with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

9. Lower the landmine in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the landmine up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

11. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Landmine Split Squat
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you the end of the barbell attached to the landmine, grabbing it with one hand

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad. The leg which is the same side as the landmine should be downed

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Landmine Squat
description:
1) While facing the landmine take up your squat position, which for most people is between shoulder and hip width apart with their feet.

2) Carefully lift the bar up or have a partner hand it to you so you position it to the front of your chest. Grab it with both hands, with elbows by your side.

3) From here, while bracing your core, start by squatting down in a controlled manner to a position your mobility allows you. If able, slightly touch the thighs/knees with your elbows or bring them slightly inside of the knees depending on foot position.

4) From the bottom position, keep your core engaged and proceed to push your feet through the floor and drive yourself back up to the start position.

5) Make sure to keep the barbell directly in front at all times.

name: Lateral Banded Dead Bug
description:
1. Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band parallel to the side of your body

2. Grab the band with clasped hands and position it in front of your chest. Next, push the band straight in the air and straighten your arms, with both legs at 90-degree angles

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Lateral Step Up
description:
1. Stand parallel to a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine. Hands can be by your side or out in front for balance

3. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

4. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

5. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

6. From here, reverse the movement by stepping down with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Leg Extension Machine
description:
1. Adjust the machine so you can sit with your back flat against the seat and your knees flush against the edge.

2. Adjust the pad so it's positioned against the front of your ankles and then choose the range of motion you would like.

3. Grab the handles and pull yourself into the machine. This helps you lock yourself into position and create a good brace.

4. Push hard against the pad and extend your legs out in front, making sure you remain in contact with the pad at all times.

5. Fully extend and squeeze your quads, holding the weight in position for at least one second.

6. Return to the start position in a controlled manner.

* I'm using a pad underneath my knees in the video to increase the range of motion as this particular machine is too small for my long ass legs!

name: Leg Lock Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your center line

2. Then bring one knee up toward the chest, at least above the waistline, and hold it in place with both hands

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked

4. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

5. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Leg Raise
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs straight and your arms straight by your side

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise both legs from the floor. As you raise your legs it’s important that you keep your back flat to the floor

4. When you reach the top, pause and then lower your legs to the start position in a slow controlled manner. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower, keeping constant tension in your core

5. Gently touch the floor with your heels and repeat

6. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

name: Levator Scapulae Stretch
description:
1. Can do stood or seated

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine and you have a nice upright posture

3. Start by turning your head to the side and placing your hand on the back of your head

4. Then carefully pull your head down towards the same side hip so you feel the stretch

5. You should feel a stretch around your lower neck/upper back area

6. Be careful not to pull down too hard and make sure you maintain posture throughout

7. Hold for allotted time

name: Loaded Chin Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a chin-up bar with a shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Loaded Close Grip Chin Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a chin-up bar so your hands are inside of your shoulders, palms facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Loaded Close Grip Pull Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside of shoulder width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Loaded Dead Bug
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degree angles

2. Carefully pick up have a partner hand you a dumbbell, kettlebell, or plate, and position above your head with your arms straight in the air

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then extend one leg towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

5. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower

6. Gently touch the floor with your heel. Pause and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core throughout

7. If you find that you lose spinal position when lowering, stop at the point this happens rather than lowering further

8. Repeat on the opposite side

name: Loaded Neutral Grip Chin Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a neutral grip chin-up bar with both hands so your palms are facing each other. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Loaded Pull Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Loaded Wide Grip Pull Up
description:
1. With a weight belt around your waist, attach a weight plate or kettlebell

2. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them wider than shoulder-width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

3. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

4. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

5. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

6. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

7. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

8. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Lock Big 3
description:
1. Lay on the floor face down. Place pillow/pad under forehead if needed for support.

2. Grab weights in hands.

3. Position 1 - Arms in 'T' position with palms facing down.

4. Raise arms up by first retracting shoulder blades and then lifting arms from floor, pausing at the top.

5. Return to the start position with control.

6. Position 2 - Arms by side with palms facing down.

7. Position 3 - Arms by side with palms facing up.

name: Lower Body Warm Up
description:
(Foam Rolling)

Foam Roller - Thoracic Spine: 30 sec
Foam Roller - Adductor: 30 sec
Foam Roller - Calf: 30 sec

(Mobility)

Kneeling Hip Opener: 30 sec
Adductor Stretch and Rotation: 10
Sprinter Calf Stretch: 30 sec
Straight Leg Swing: 10 per leg
Side to Side Leg Swings: 10 per leg
3 Part Shoulder Warm-Up: 10 of each

(Activation)

Clamshell: 1 set x 20
Single-Leg Glute Bridge: 1 set x 10 per leg
Bird Dog: 1 set x 5 per side
Elbow Side Plank: 2 sets x 2 x 15 secs

name: McGill Curl Up
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with one knee bent (foot flat) and the other leg straight (toes pointed up)

2. Your head should be on the floor and then place your hands underneath your lower back (this ensures you keep a neutral spine). Your elbows should be on the floor

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and raising your head off the floor a few inches and holding this position

4. The aim is to keep your lower back from bending when you raise up. You shouldn’t push your head forward at any time

5. Raise your elbows from the floor to decrease stability and increase the difficulty

name: McGill Curl Up Isometric
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with one knee bent (foot flat) and the other leg straight (toes pointed up)

2. Your head should be on the floor and then place your hands underneath your lower back (this ensures you keep a neutral spine). Your elbows should be on the floor

3. Begin the movement by bracing your core and raising your head off the floor a few inches and holding this position

4. The aim is to keep your lower back from bending when you raise up. You shouldn’t push your head forward at any time

5. Raise your elbows from the floor to decrease stability and increase the difficulty

6. Maintain position for allotted time, keeping constant tension in core

name: Medicine Ball Plank
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Position your hands flat on a medicine ball and create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Medicine Ball Plank Alternating Knee To Chest
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Position your hands flat on a medicine ball and create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Begin the move by bringing one knee in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep a neutral spine in the process

6. You can either touch the floor as you bring your knee in or keep it elevated

7. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

9. Repeat on the other side

name: Medicine Ball Plank Knee To Chest
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Position your hands flat on a medicine ball and create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Begin the move by bringing one knee in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep a neutral spine in the process

6. You can either touch the floor as you bring your knee in or keep it elevated. The same goes for when you return your leg

7. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Medicine Ball Push Up.
description:
1) Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

2) Place one of your hands on top of a medicine ball

3) Extend one leg back at a time and assume a push up position with the legs straight, arm straight on the floor, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

4) Grip the floor and ball by twisting your hands in place

5) With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself by retracting the shoulder blades and bending at the elbows

6) Descend as low as possible, while keeping the correct position throughout

7) From this position drive your body back up to the starting point while keeping constant tension throughout the chest, core, and glutes

name: Medicine Ball Slam From Knees
description:
1. Take a full kneeling position on the floor with a slam ball in both hands

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Start the movement by bracing your core and bringing the ball above your head (keeping a neutral spine)

4. Then from here explosively slam the ball towards the floor just in front of you, finishing with your arms straight and your core engaged

5. Carefully pick the ball up and repeat

name: Mini Band Leg Extension
description:
1. Take up a seated position on a bench or chair and place a band around one of your ankles and around the other foot, feet flat

2. Engage your core and make sure you have a neutral spine with an upright posture

3. Place the foot with the band underneath firmly on the floor and then raise the other foot by extending your leg

4. Movement should come solely from the knee, so limit any movement elsewhere

5. Finish with your leg parallel to the floor. Squeeze your quad (upper thigh) in this position

6. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

name: Narrow EZ Bar Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you an EZ bar and hold it with a narrow grip

3. Place your arms straight by your side and brace your core. Make sure you have a neutral spine, and your arms aren’t locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the bar up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the bar, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Negative Chin Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a chin-up bar with a shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Negative Close Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a chin-up bar so your hands are inside of your shoulders, palms facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Negative Close Grip Pull Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip so your hands are inside of your shoulders. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Negative Neutral Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a neutral grip chin-up bar with both hands so your palms are facing each other

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Negative Pull Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Negative Wide Grip Pull Up
description:
1. Stand on a step, bench, etc to raise yourself up. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them wider than shoulder-width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by either carefully stepping off or jumping up towards the bar so your chin is above the bar. Your shoulder blades should be depressed down

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Start by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats, core, and upper back

6. Keep your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. When your arms are straight, either step back on the step or carefully drop to the floor

8. The goal of this exercise is to lower yourself as slow as possible

name: Neutral Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Take up a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Neutral Grip Chin Up
description:
1. Grasp a neutral grip chin-up bar with both hands so your palms are facing each other. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

6. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Neutral Grip TRX Row
description:
1. Face a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Straighten your arms so the slack is taken off the straps

2. Palms facing each other in a neutral grip with eyes looking forward

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with an upright posture. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement. Feet can be flat or on heels

4. Take a step in with both feet while keeping the arms straight (the more parallel your body is to the floor the greater the difficulty, so start off more upright)

5. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades. Quickly pull yourself up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Shoulders should not elevate up towards the ears at any point

8. Pause and then lower yourself to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
description:
1. Set up in a Nordic hamstring machine or on a lat pulldown machine. Make sure you have an upright posture and a neutral spine

2. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, with your hands in front of your chest

3. Begin by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner while keeping your hips extended and core engaged. Lower yourself as low as possible until you can’t control the descent anymore

4. Keep constant tension in your hamstrings and glutes as you lower

5. At this point, allow yourself to fall to the floor in a controlled fashion and carefully place your hands on the floor to stop yourself

6. Return to the start position by giving yourself just enough of a push off the floor so you can use your hamstrings to pull yourself up

7. Repeat

name: Offset Carry - Kettlebell Suitcase, Rack
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in both hands

2. Clean one of the kettlebells into a front rack position, locking your arm in tight to your torso. Position the other kettlebell straight by your side, ensuring it doesn't touch the side of your body

3. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Overhand Machine Row
description:
1. Sit with your chest against the pad and grab a handle in each hand.

2. Begin by retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows back.

3. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point and pause slightly.

4. Return to the start position by reversing the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your upper back.

name: Partner Assisted Nordic Hamstring Curl
description:
1. Set up in a full-kneeling position with an upright posture and a neutral spine

2. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, with your hands in front of your chest

3. Have a partner hold your ankles down and use their body weight to counter yours

4. Begin by lowering yourself towards the floor in a slow controlled manner while keeping your hips extended and core engaged. Lower yourself as low as possible until you can’t control the descent anymore

5. Keep constant tension in your hamstrings and glutes as you lower

6. At this point, allow yourself to fall to the floor in a controlled fashion and carefully place your hands on the floor to stop yourself

7. Return to the start position by giving yourself just enough of a push off the floor so you can use your hamstrings to pull yourself up

8. Repeat

name: Pendlay Row
description:
1. Stand behind a barbell with your feet shoulder-width apart

2. Start by bracing your core and hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows. Then grab the barbell wider than shoulder-width apart with an overhand grip (blocks can be used to elevate bar up)

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking down

4. Begin by retracting your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Plank - Shoulders 120 Degrees
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Then go onto your forearms, positioning them further forward than a regular plank so there's a 120-degree angle in your shoulders

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

6. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

7. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Plank Arm Reach
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Then go onto your forearms and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

6. Begin by extending one arm and reaching out in front. Pause and return the arm in a slow controlled manner

7. As you reach you want to minimize any movement in your body and keep your core engaged

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Plank Band Kickback
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Anchor a band around a secure surface such as a dumbbell and grasp it with one hand

4. Then get into a straight arm plank position. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Next, bring the arm with the band parallel with your torso and then place it at a ninety-degree angle

6. Start the movement by pushing the band backward and extending your arm, ensuring you keep your upper arm stationary at all times

7. Stop just short of locking out your elbow and pause slightly, contracting your triceps. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your triceps and core

8. Keep a neutral spine throughout, making sure to stop any rotation in your torso

name: Plank Cable Row
description:
1. Attach a handle to a cable pulley positioned at the bottom

2. Facing the cable machine, set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Then go onto your forearms and grab a handle with one hand

4. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Start by depressing your shoulder blade and then pulling your elbow in towards your side in a controlled manner

7. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your Lats and core

name: Plank On Knees
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Then go onto your forearms and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then push one knee back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged. The further back your knees, the greater the difficulty

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and knees roughly hip-width apart

5. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

6. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

7. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips up

name: Plate Push Up
description:
1) Carefully lift a plate onto your upper back or have a partner lift it up

2) Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

3) Extend one leg back at a time and assume a push up position with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

4) Grip the floor by twisting your hands in place

5) With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by retracting the shoulder blades and bending at the elbows

6) Descend until the upper arms are parallel or your chest touches the floor

7) From this position drive your body back up to the starting point while keeping constant tension throughout the chest, core, and glutes

name: Pronated Bent Over Row
description:
1. Stand behind a barbell with your feet shoulder-width apart

2. Carefully lift the barbell up either by deadlifting it or lifting it up from a rack with an overhand grip, wider than shoulder-width apart

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Pronated Grip TRX Row
description:
1. Face a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Straighten your arms so the slack is taken off the straps

2. Palms facing down in a pronated grip with eyes looking forward

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with an upright posture. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement. Feet can be flat or on heels

4. Take a step in with both feet while keeping the arms straight (the more parallel your body is to the floor the greater the difficulty, so start off more upright)

5. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades. Quickly pull yourself up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows back so hands finish to the outside of your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Shoulders should not elevate up towards the ears at any point

8. Pause and then lower yourself to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back

name: Pronated to Neutral Grip TRX Row
description:
1. Face a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Straighten your arms so the slack is taken off the straps

2. Palms facing down in a pronated grip with eyes looking forward

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with an upright posture. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement. Feet can be flat or on heels

4. Take a step in with both feet while keeping the arms straight (the more parallel your body is to the floor the greater the difficulty, so start off more upright)

5. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades. Quickly pull yourself up in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side and rotating your hands so they finish with palms facing each other

6. Hands should finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

7. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

8. Shoulders should not elevate up towards the ears at any point

9. Pause and then lower yourself to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back

name: Prone 'T' - Elbows 90 Degrees
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Position your arms at 90-degree angles and ensure you have a neutral spine, with your hands below the bench and your palms facing up towards you

3. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then retract your shoulder blades and raise your arms while keeping them at a 90-degree angle

4. Go as high as your range of motion allows, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

5. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Prone Back Extension
description:
1. Get into a prone position on the floor, lying on your stomach with your legs straight and feet together

2. Your head should be facing the floor and your hands by the side of your head. Ensure you have a neutral spine and your shoulder blades are pulled back

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your chest from the floor in a controlled manner, going as high as you can while maintaining the correct form

4. As you raise up ensure you avoid any movement in your legs and upper back

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor throughout the movement

6. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lower back and glutes

name: Prone Cobra
description:
1) Starting Position: Begin by lying face down on an exercise mat or a flat, comfortable surface. Extend your legs straight and keep them together. Place your arms alongside your body with your palms facing upwards.

2) Set-Up: Position your forehead or chin gently on the mat, depending on what feels more comfortable for your neck. Keep your neck and spine in a neutral position throughout the exercise.

3) Activation: Engage your core muscles by pulling your belly button slightly towards your spine. This will help stabilize your lower back and pelvis during the movement.

Movement:

1) Raise your chest, head, and arms off the mat, while keeping your legs in contact with the floor.

2) At the top of the movement, your upper body should form a slight curve, resembling a cobra raising its head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together to activate your upper back muscles fully. Maintain a comfortable distance between your chin and chest to avoid straining your neck.

3) Hold the position for 2-3 seconds, focusing on the contraction in your back and shoulder muscles.

4) Keep breathing steadily throughout the hold.

5) Gradually lower your chest, head, and arms back to the starting position with control.

name: Prone Dumbbell Overhead Press On Bench
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Grasp a dumbbell in each hand and position your arms at 90-degree angles. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Get into the starting position by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then retract your shoulder blades and row the dumbbells up, externally rotating at the top so your arms are parallel with the floor

4. Begin the movement by then pressing the dumbbells overhead while keeping them as parallel to the floor as possible, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

5. Bring the dumbbells towards each other as you press forward

6. As you press the dumbbells, make sure your shoulders don't elevate up towards your ears

7. When arms are fully extended, pause slightly and then bring the dumbbells back in a slow controlled manner by pulling your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Prone Overhead Press On Bench
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Position your arms at 90-degree angles and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Get into the starting position by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then retract your shoulder blades and row the dumbbells up, externally rotating at the top so your arms are parallel with the floor

4. Begin the movement by then extending your arms while keeping them as parallel to the floor as possible, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

5. Bring your hands towards each other as you press forward

6. As you extend your arms make sure your shoulders don't elevate up towards your ears

7. When arms are fully extended, pause slightly and then bring your arms back in a slow controlled manner by pulling your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Prone Shoulder External Rotation At 90 Degree
description:
1. Get into a prone position on a flat bench, lying on your stomach with your feet secure on the floor

2. Grasp a dumbbell in one hand and position your arm at a 90-degree angle, the other hand braced against the floor. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Get into the starting position by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then retract your shoulder blade and bring the dumbbell from the floor so your arm is at a 90-degree angle

4. In this position then externally rotate your arm so it finishes parallel with the floor

5. Make sure to keep your shoulders depressed down and your shoulder blades squeezed

6. The movement should come solely from the shoulder, so avoid any movement in your wrist

7. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders

name: Pull Up
description:
1. Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them shoulder-width apart. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

3. If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

4. Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

5. Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

6. Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

7. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

name: Push Up From Knees
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

3. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

4. Slightly push one knee back at a time with the arms straight and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

5. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

6. Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

7. From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

name: Push Up.
description:
1. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

2. Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

3. Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

4. Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

5. With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

6. Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

7. From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

name: Quadruped 'T'
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by externally rotating one arm so your palm is facing forward and your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be out to the side

4. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

5. Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

6. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

7. Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

name: Quadruped 'Y'
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Begin the movement by externally rotating one arm so your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be positioned at a 45-degree angle, a Y position

4. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

5. Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

6. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

7. Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

name: Quadruped Band 'T'
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Place a band under one of your hands and grasp with the other, ensuring enough resistance

4. Begin the movement by externally rotating the arm with the band so your palm is facing forward and your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be out to the side

4. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

5. Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

6. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

7. Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

name: Quadruped Band 'Y'
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Place a band under one of your hands and grasp with the other, ensuring enough resistance

4. Begin the movement by externally rotating the arm with the band so your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be positioned at a 45-degree angle, a Y position

5. With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

5. Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

6. Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

7. Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

name: Quadruped Band Kickback
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

3. Anchor a band around a secure surface such as a dumbbell and grasp it with one hand

4. Bring this arm parallel with your torso and then place it at a ninety-degree angle

5. Start the movement by pushing the band backward and extending your arm, ensuring you keep your upper arm stationary at all times

6. Stop just short of locking out your elbow and pause slightly, contracting your triceps. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your triceps and core

7. Keep a neutral spine throughout, making sure to stop any rotation in your torso

name: Quadruped Banded Scapular Protraction
description:
1. Put each hand in either end of a resistance band and place it around your back so it wraps around the upper back area

2. Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

3. Twist your triceps into your armpits, this will stop your pecs from taking over

4. From this position, push your body away from the floor while keeping your triceps twisted into your armpits

5. Pause slightly at the top and then reverse the movement by bringing your body down towards the floor while keeping your arms straight

6. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and then repeat the movement

7. Make sure to keep your head in a neutral position looking at the floor at all times, ensuring no movement comes from your neck

name: Quadruped T Spine Rotations
description:
1. Get into a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

2. Engage your core and make sure the spine is in a neutral position

3. Place one hand in the middle of your upper back or the back of the neck

4. Start by rotating your torso and bringing your elbow down as low as possible towards the opposite hand in a controlled manner

5. Your eyes, head, and shoulder should all move as one as you rotate

6. Then reverse the motion leading with the eyes, head, and shoulder rotating upwards as far as possible

7. Maintain the position of the downed arm and keep your hips and legs from moving as you rotate

name: Quadruped T Spine Rotations - Lumbar Locked (Roller)
description:
1. Get into a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

2. Then place a foam roller on the back of your calves and sit back onto it, keeping your hands under your shoulders

3. Engage your core and make sure the spine is in a neutral position

4. Place one hand in the middle of your upper back or the back of the neck

5. Start by rotating your torso and bringing your elbow down as low as possible towards the opposite hand in a controlled manner

6. Your eyes, head, and shoulder should all move as one as you rotate

7. Then reverse the motion leading with the eyes, head, and shoulder rotating upwards as far as possible

8. Maintain the position of the downed arm and pressure on the roller as you rotate

name: Rack Assisted Squat
description:
1) Stand in-front of a pillar.

2) Position feet in squat stance and wrap hands around pillar as shown in clip.

3) Squat down to a position that you're comfortable with by using the pillar for assistance.

4) When you get to the bottom of the rep, pause slightly, squeeze your glutes and stand back up.

5) The closer you stand to the pillar the greater the difficulty.

name: Rack Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Then clean them up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be at a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

9. Repeat with the opposite leg

name: Rack Kettlebell Split Squat
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand. Then clean them up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Rack Single Kettlebell Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Position the kettlebell in a rack position at the front of the shoulder. The kettlebell should be resting on the outside of the forearm, elbow pointing down, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Rack Single Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand. Then clean it up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg that is on the same side as the kettlebell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be at a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Rack Single Kettlebell Split Squat
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand. Then clean it up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position by downing the knee on the same side as the kettlebell. Both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, and glutes

name: Rear Foot Elevated Single DB Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should either be facing laces down or have the toes pointed down into the step. Arms by your side

4. Pick up a dumbbell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Rear Foot Elevated Single KB Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should either be facing laces down or have the toes pointed down into the step. Arms by your side

4. Pick up a kettlebell in the hand that’s the same side as the downed knee

5. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

6. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

7. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat
description:
1. Position yourself with your back to a plate, pad or step (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position with the foot of the downed knee elevated. The back leg will be at less of an angle to the front leg, which should be near 90-degrees. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should either be facing laces down or have the toes pointed down into the step. Arms by your side

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Reverse Crunch On Bench
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on a bench. Brace yourself in position by holding the top of the bench with both hands. with both legs at 90-degrees and your heels touching the floor

2. Next, place your legs at 90-degree angles and position them so they’re just below the bench

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

4. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep your legs at the same angle

5. As you bring your knees in, raise your hips from the bench while keeping your core engaged and your spine neutral

6. Pause in this position and contract your abs, then return your hips to the bench and lower your legs back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

7. Lower your feet past the level of the bench and then repeat

name: Reverse Crunch on Bench with Roller
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on a bench and place a foam roller behind your knees, holding it in position by bringing your legs together

2. Brace yourself in position by holding the top of the bench with both hands. with both legs at 90-degrees and your heels touching the floor

3. Next, place your legs at 90-degree angles and position them so they’re just below the bench

4. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

5. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep your legs at the same angle so the roller stays in place

6. As you bring your knees in, raise your hips from the bench while keeping your core engaged and your spine neutral

7. Pause in this position and contract your abs, then return your hips to the bench and lower your legs back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Lower your feet past the level of the bench and then repeat

name: Reverse Crunch With Roller
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor and place a foam roller behind your knees, holding it in position by bringing your legs together

2. Place your arms straight by your side

3. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat
