2. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep your legs at the same angle so the roller stays in place

4. As you bring your knees in, raise your hips from the floor while keeping your core engaged and your spine neutral

5. Pause in this position and contract your abs, then return your hips to the floor and lower your legs back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

6. Gently touch the floor and then repeat

name: Reverse Crunch.
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degrees and your heels touching the floor. Place your arms straight by your side

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

1. Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep your legs at the same angle

3. As you bring your knees in, raise your hips from the floor while keeping your core engaged and your spine neutral

4. Pause in this position and contract your abs, then return your hips to the floor and lower your legs back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

5. Gently touch the floor and then repeat

name: Reverse Lunge
description:
1) Start with feet hip-width apart and facing forward.

2) Start by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle. Keep on the toes with the back foot.

3) Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force.

4) The front leg should also finish in a 90-degree angle, while the foot should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement.

5) From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot while maintaining an upright torso. Make sure to keep constant tension in the core throughout the movement.

name: Reverse Nordic Curl
description:
1. Assume a full kneeling position on the floor, with your knees on a mat

2. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then slowly lower yourself backward in a slow controlled manner

3. Keep your hips extended and make sure you keep a neutral spine as you lower. Your body position from your knees to your head shouldn't change at any time during the movement

4. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause slightly and then reverse the movement by contracting your quads and pushing forward

5. As you move forward, make sure your hips stay extended and your back straight

name: RKC Plank
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Then go onto your forearms and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

4. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

5. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

6. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

7. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: RNT Split Squat (Lateral)
description:
1. Stand parallel to a band and wrap it around the knee closest to it

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position by placing the knee without the band on the floor, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

7. The emphasis is on keeping the front knee in line with your foot at all times

name: RNT Split Squat (Medial)
description:
1. Stand parallel to a band and wrap it around the knee furthest away to it

2. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position by placing the knee without the band on the floor, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

3. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

4. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

5. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

6. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

7. The emphasis is on keeping the front knee in line with your foot at all times

name: Rope Neutral Curl
description:
1. Attach a rope to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the rope with a neutral grip and take a step back with both feet

2. Position your arms straight by your side and fix your upper arms to your sides. Your arms should be slightly in front of you

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your upper arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the rope up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl up the rope, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Rope Pressdown
description:
1. Facing a cable machine, set up a rope at the top and grab with both hands

2. The cable should be directly in front of you

3. Start with your arms bent and fixed into your side. Your upper arms should be perpendicular with the floor and should stay so for the entire movement

4. Keep your shoulders pinned down and engage your core

5. Begin the movement by pressing the rope down and extending your arms until straight, but not fully locked out

6. Keep your elbows fixed and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause at the bottom and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Rope Supinating Curl
description:
1. Attach a rope to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the rope with a neutral grip and take a step back with both feet

2. Position your arms straight by your side and fix your upper arms to your sides. Your arms should be slightly in front of you

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your upper arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the rope up in a controlled fashion

5. As your curling up, rotate your hands so you finish with your palms facing you. Only begin supinating your grip when you reach 90-degrees with your arms

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl up the rope, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner by reversing the movement, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Seated Alternating Arnold Press
description:
1) Take a seated position with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility).

2) With a dumbbell in each hand, position them at the front of the shoulder with the palm facing in towards the chest.

3) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, drive one of the dumbbells up.

4) As you press it up, externally rotate the dumbbell so you finish with your palm facing forward away from you.

5) Ensure the shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

6) Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders and core.

7) Repeat with the other side in an alternating fashion.

name: Seated Alternating Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Seated Alternating Dumbbell Neutral Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Take up a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Seated Alternating Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Take a seated position with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility).

2) With a dumbbell in each hand, position them at the top of the shoulders with the palm of your hands facing inwards toward the midline of the body.

3) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, drive one of the dumbbells up.

4) Ensure the shoulder doesn't elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

5) Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders and core.

6) Repeat on the other side in an alternating fashion.

name: Seated Alternating Dumbbell Zottman Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbell in, so your palm is facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep, forearm, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

name: Seated Arnold Press
description:
1) Take a seated position with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility).

2) With a dumbbell in each hand, position them at the front of the shoulder with the palm facing in towards the chest.

3) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, drive the dumbbells up.

4) As you press them up, externally rotate the dumbbells so you finish with your palms facing forwards away from you.

5) Ensure the shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

6) Proceed to lower the dumbbells back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders and core.

name: Seated Barbell Press
description:
1) Take a seated position in a rack with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility

2) Barbell should be slightly behind you and just above your head in the rack

3) Grasp the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart

4) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, carefully push the barbell up and bring it forward so it's above your head

5) Start by lowering the barbell in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders and core

6) Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to your body as you're lowering the bar, bringing it to the top of your chest

7) From this position pause slightly and then reverse the movement by pressing the bar up to the start position in a controlled manner

8) Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during the pressing movement. Your back should stay in contact with the bench at all times and your spine should stay neutral

name: Seated Bent-Over Dumbbell Reverse Fly
description:
1. Sat on a bench, grasp a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip

2. Retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down

3. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows, your arms should be straight but with your elbows soft

4. Your back should be straight and your spine in a neutral position

5. Then raise the dumbbells to the side by driving your elbows back and raising the dumbbells. Your arms should be at or near a 90-degree angle to your body

6. Ensure your shoulders don't raise up towards your ears

7. Pause and squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

8. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, upper back, and shoulders while maintaining a neutral spine

9. Be careful not to bend or straighten your arms as you raise the dumbbells up and down, ensuring the same angle throughout. Make sure you don't use any momentum or swinging to raise the dumbbells

name: Seated Dowel Thoracic Rotations
description:
1. Seated, position a dowel across the top of your back and grab with both hands

2. You should have a neutral spine with your head facing forward and your chest up

3. Keep your core engaged and then start by rotating your upper body as far to one side as you can in a controlled manner

4. The movement should be coming from the upper body so there should be no movement in your hips or lower body whatsoever

5. Pause for a second when you reach the end of your range of motion, then exhale as you return to the start position

name: Seated Dual Cable Row
description:
1. Position a bench in front of a cable machine and attach two handles. Adjust the height so handles are just below your chest when sat

2. Sit and grab a handle in each hand, with your palms facing each other. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so your hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Dual Pulley Face Pull
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach two handles so they’re at chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab a handle with each hand with an overhand grip. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the handles in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the handles apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Dumbbell Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Seated Dumbbell Frontal Raise
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with your feet shoulder-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand. You can either position your back against the bench or have it flat with no support

2. Dumbbells should be by your side with your palms facing your body. Ensure your arms don’t rest on your sides

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core, then raise the dumbbells in front of you by bringing your arms forward, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your shoulders at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the front of your shoulders and core

6. Dumbbells shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Seated Dumbbell Lateral Raise
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with your feet shoulder-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand. You can either position your back against the bench or have it flat with no support

2. Dumbbells should be by your side with your palms facing your body. Ensure your arms don’t rest on your sides

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and raising your arms to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your elbows at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the side of your shoulders and core

6. Dumbbells shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Seated Dumbbell Neutral Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Take up a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

8. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Seated Dumbbell Neutral Grip Press
description:
1) Take a seated position with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility.

2) With a dumbbell in each hand, position them at the top of the shoulders with the palm of your hands facing inwards toward the midline of the body.

3) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, drive the dumbbells up.

4) Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

5) Proceed to lower the dumbbells back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders and core.

name: Seated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in both hands. Bench should ideally have a small back support but not 100% necessary

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine, then lift the dumbbells above your head so your arms are straight but not fully locked out

3. Your upper arms should be beside your ears and the dumbbells should be in a neutral position, palms facing in

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner, bringing them towards the middle of your shoulder blades

5. Ensure your upper arms stay fixed throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Seated Dumbbell Triceps Extension - Pronating
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in both hands. Bench should ideally have a small back support but not 100% necessary

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine, then lift the dumbbells above your head so your arms are straight but not fully locked out

3. Your upper arms should be beside your ears and the dumbbells should be in a pronated position, palms facing away from you

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands in as you lower the dumbbells, finishing in a neutral position with your palms facing in

5. Bring them towards the middle of your shoulder blades and ensure your upper arms stay fixed throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Seated EZ Bar Triceps Extension
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with an EZ bar in both hands. Bench should ideally have a small back support but not 100% necessary

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine, then lift the bar above your head so your arms are straight but not fully locked out

3. Your upper arms should be beside your ears

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the bar in a slow controlled manner

5. Ensure your upper arms stay fixed throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Seated Neutral Grip Cable Row
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a v-bar so it’s just below chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab a handle with each hand. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle towards the bottom of the chest in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Neutral Grip Face Pull
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach two handles so they’re at chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab a handle with each hand with a neutral grip. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the handles in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the handles apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Rope Face Pull
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

5. At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart so they finish to the side of your head. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Rope Row
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s just below chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the rope with an overhand grip with each hand. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the rope towards the bottom of the chest in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Single Arm Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Take a seated position with your back against the bench (bench angle usually between 60-75 degrees, depending on shoulder mobility).

2) Grab a dumbbell and position it at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body.

3) From this position, while pushing the feet down into the floor and engaging your core, drive the dumbbell up.

4) Ensure the shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement.

5) Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder and core.

name: Seated Single Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
1. Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in one hand. Bench should ideally have a small back support but not 100% necessary

2. Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine, then lift the dumbbell above your head so your arm is straight but not fully locked out

3. Your upper arm should be beside your ear and the dumbbell should be in a neutral position, palm facing in

4. Begin the movement by bending your elbow and lowering the dumbbell in a slow controlled manner, bringing it towards the middle of your shoulder blades

5. Ensure your upper arms stay fixed throughout the entire movement

6. When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Seated Single Cable Pronated Row
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a handle so it’s just below chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the handle with an overhand grip in one hand. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner in line with the top of the chest by rowing your elbow back and out to your side

5. Arm should finish almost parallel to the floor and in a 90-degree angle. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your middle back, rather than just your arm

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Single Cable Row
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a handle so it’s just below chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the handle with a neutral grip in one hand. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Straight Bar Row
description:
1. Position a flat bench in front of a cable machine and attach a straight bar so it’s just below chest height when sat

2. Sit with an upright posture and grab the bar with an overhand grip with each hand. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the bar towards the bottom of the chest in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Seated Thoracic Rotation and Flexion
description:
1. Take a seat

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine and you have a nice upright posture

3. Start by giving yourself the biggest hug possible by wrapping your arms around yourself

4. Start by bending forward and then rotating as far to one side as you can. When you cant go any further, stop and then pull yourself towards the ground

5. Hold and then return to the start position

6. Repeat going in the opposite side

7. You will feel a stretch in your upper and middle back

name: Seated Zottman Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Position your palms facing forwards

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the dumbbells up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl them as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

6. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbells, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbells in, so your palms are facing down

8. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps, forearms, and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

name: Short Banded Barbell Hip Thrust
description:
1. If using a rack, attach two mini bands and hook around each end of a barbell. If not, attach the bands to two heavy dumbbells instead

2. Then secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

3. Next, while seated, roll the padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

4. With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

8. Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Shoulder Rolls
description:
1. Can do stood, seated, or on knees

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine and you have a nice upright posture, with your arms straight by your side

3. You're going to make circles with your shoulders, moving them clockwise and anti-clockwise.

4. Start by first bringing them up, then back, then down, and then forward. Repeat sequence.

5. Perform for allotted reps and then repeat going the opposite way

6. The aim is to create as much movement in your shoulder blades as possible

name: Shoulder Taps
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

3. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

4. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

5. Then lift one hand off the floor and touch the opposite shoulder. Pause and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

6. Aim is to resist any rotation in your body when you lift your hand. Keep constant tension in your core and glutes

7. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Side Plank - Knee/Hip Abduction Lift
description:
1. Lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. Position your top foot on top of the bench with the other foot below

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line, using your top foot against the bench to assist

6. Raise your bottom leg as you drive your hips up so it finishes parallel with the opposite leg

7. Pause in this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

8. Return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep a neutral spine throughout

name: Side Plank With Cable/Band Row
description:
1. Position a handle about two feet from the floor on a cable machine. Then lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched.

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Grab the handle with your hand and straighten your arm out in front

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Next, retract your shoulder blade and then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Side-Lying Hip Adduction
description:
1. Lie on your side and support your head with your hand. Place your other hand flat on the floor or on your hips

2. Your head should be facing forward and you should have a neutral spine

3. With your legs straight, bend the top leg and bring it up the mat, placing your foot flat on the floor just in front of the other knee (or as close as mobility allows)

4. Begin the movement by posteriorly tilting your pelvis (tucking in your tailbone) and raising the bottom leg as high as possible while keeping it straight

5. Pause slightly at the top and then reverse the movement by lowering your leg in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the inside of your thigh and your core

name: Side-Lying Rotation - Arm Straight
description:
1. Lie on your back on the floor with your legs straight and your arms flat by your side

2. Then bring your knees into your chest and drop both to one side so outer knee is touching the floor

3. Grab the top leg with the opposite hand and place your other arm across your body so your upper body comes off the floor.

4. Your head should now be facing to the side

5. Start by rotating your arm by reaching over your shoulder. Your arm should stay straight

6. As you move, your upper body/back should go towards the floor and your head should rotate to the other side

7. Keep your legs in the same position throughout

8. Exhale as you rotate back

name: Singe-Leg Calf Isometrics
description:
To start, raise both heels first.

Then lift one foot off the floor so you're standing on one leg.

Return to the floor using both feet.

Repeat on other side.

name: Single Arm Decline Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set up on a decline bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up with one hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbell up into the air so the arm is straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbell at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbell until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core, as well as the free hand.

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Floor Press
description:
1) Take up a seated position on the floor and pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbell up into the air so the arm is straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbell at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbell with the arm are at a 45-degree angle to the body until your elbow touches the floor.

4) Control the dumbbell down and be careful not to bang your elbow off the floor.

5) While keeping your back flat to the floor, proceed to push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

6) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Renegade Row
description:
1. Position yourself next to a step and get into a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Place one hand on the step and grab a dumbbell with the other

2. Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

3. Get into the start position by straitening both legs. Arms should be straight, eyes looking down and core braced

4. Begin by retracting your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the dumbbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Movement through the body should be limited to the moving arm, with the hips and spine staying as stationary as possible

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning the dumbbell to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Row
description:
1) Assume a kneeling position on the bench with your same side hand braced against the bench.

2) The foot on the floor should be in a stable position.

3) Brace your core and carefully pick up a dumbbell with the opposite hand with a neutral grip.

4) Begin the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and bringing the dumbbell up by driving the elbow back.

5) Pull the dumbbell up towards the body, in a slight arcing motion, until your elbow is level (or just past) with your torso. The dumbbell should finish towards your hip with your arm in a ninety-degree angle.

6) At the top of the movement, pause and squeeze your back muscles.

7) Proceed to then reverse the movement and lower the weight in a slow, controlled manner.

8) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, upper back and core.

name: Single Arm Flat Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set up on a flat bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up with one hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbell up into the air so the arm is straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbell at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbell until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core, as well as the free hand.

name: Single Arm Incline Dumbbell Press
description:
1) Set a bench up to an incline, typically between 15-30 degrees and then carefully pick a dumbbell up with one hand.

2) Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbell up into the air so the arm is straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbell at a 45-degree angle.

3) Begin by slowly lowering the dumbbell until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

4) Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

5) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core, as well as the free hand.

name: Single Arm Kettlebell Deadlift
description:
1. Place a kettlebell on a stool or platform and stand over it with the feet approximately shoulder-width apart

2. Begin in a neutral stance position with the ear, shoulder, hip, and knee in proper alignment with the arms resting comfortably in front

3. From here, while bracing your core, place one hand behind your back

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and then bend your knees so you can then grasp the kettlebell with an overhand grip

5. From here pull the kettlebell up in a straight line by pushing your feet through the floor and lifting the kettlebell from the platform

6. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

7. Lower the kettlebell by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the kettlebell lower slide in a straight vertical line

name: Single Arm Kettlebell Renegade Row
description:
1. Position yourself next to a step and get into a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Place one hand on the step and grab a kettlebell with the other

2. Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

3. Get into the start position by straitening both legs. Arms should be straight, eyes looking down and core braced

4. Begin by retracting your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the kettlebell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Movement through the body should be limited to the moving arm, with the hips and spine staying as stationary as possible

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning the kettlebell to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Single Arm Kettlebell Row
description:
1) Assume a kneeling position on the bench with your same side hand braced against the bench. The foot on the floor should be in a stable position.

2) Brace your core and carefully pick up a kettlebell with the opposite hand with a neutral grip.

3) Begin the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and bringing the kettlebell up by driving the elbow back.

4) Pull the kettlebell up towards the body, in a slight arcing motion, until your elbow is level (or just past) with your torso. The kettlebell should finish towards your hip with your arm in a ninety-degree angle.

5) At the top of the movement, pause and squeeze your back muscles.

6) Proceed to then reverse the movement and lower the weight in a slow, controlled manner.

7) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, upper back and core.

name: Single Arm Upside Down KB press
Single Curl To Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder-width stance with your feet

2. Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral grip. Position your palms facing the midline of the body

3. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

4. With your arm locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending your elbow and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

5. Curl it up to the top of your shoulder and then from this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbell above the head

6. Make sure the dumbbell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

7. Ensure the shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during the pressing movement

8. Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core, bicep, and glutes

name: Single Dumbbell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg the same side as the dumbbell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Dumbbell Lateral Step Up
description:
1. Stand parallel to a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in the hand that is furthest away from the box and have your arms by your side

3. Place the inside foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping down with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Single Dumbbell Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with the leg that is on the opposite side to the dumbbell. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Dumbbell Neutral Scott Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 60-degree angle and position yourself behind it with a dumbbell in one hand

2. Turn the side of your body that has the dumbbell towards the bench and split your stance, with the opposite foot going back

3. Place the back of your upper arm that has the dumbbell on the bench and straighten until it’s just short of being locked out. The top of the bench should be just below your armpit

4. Brace yourself in position by placing your other hand on the bench for stability and plant your feet into the floor. Brace your core

5. Position the dumbbell in a neutral position, palm facing in towards your body

6. Begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

7. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed on the bench

8. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell or lift your upper arm from the bench. Be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

9. Pause when you reach this point then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep

10. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Dumbbell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand a dumbbell to you.

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg the same side as the dumbbell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Dumbbell Reverse Lunge/Overhead Press
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand. Then bring it up to the top of your shoulder and hold it in a neutral grip, palm facing the midline of the body

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg that is on the same side as the dumbbell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be at a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, pause and drive the dumbbell straight up

9. Ensure the shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during the pressing movement

10. Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core, and glutes.

11. Return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Dumbbell Scott Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 60-degree angle and position yourself behind it with a dumbbell in one hand

2. Turn the side of your body that has the dumbbell towards the bench and split your stance, with the opposite foot going back

3. Place the back of your upper arm that has the dumbbell on the bench and straighten until it’s just short of being locked out. The top of the bench should be just below your armpit

4. Brace yourself in position by placing your other hand on the bench for stability and plant your feet into the floor. Brace your core

5. Begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed on the bench

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell or lift your upper arm from the bench. Be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Dumbbell Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you dumbbell in one hand and have arms by your side

3. Place the foot that’s opposite to the dumbbell flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Single Dumbbell Zottman Scott Curl
description:
1. Set up a bench at a 60-degree angle and position yourself behind it with a dumbbell in one hand

2. Turn the side of your body that has the dumbbell towards the bench and split your stance, with the opposite foot going back

3. Place the back of your upper arm that has the dumbbell on the bench and straighten until it’s just short of being locked out. The top of the bench should be just below your armpit

4. Brace yourself in position by placing your other hand on the bench for stability and plant your feet into the floor. Brace your core

5. Position the dumbbell in a neutral position, palm facing in towards your body

6. Begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

7. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed on the bench

8. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell or lift your upper arm from the bench. Be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

9. Pause when you reach this point and then rotate the dumbbell in, so your palm is facing down

10. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep, forearm, and core

11. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Handle Cable Curl
description:
1. Attach a handle to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the handle in one hand with an underhand grip and take a step back with both feet

2. Next, split your stance by taking a small step back with the leg on the same side as the handle. Your arm should be slightly in front of you

3. Fix your upper arm to your side

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

5. With your upper arm locked in position, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the handle up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in position

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl up the handle, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Handle Neutral Cable Curl
description:
1. Attach a handle to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the handle in one hand with a neutral grip palm facing in towards the body and take a step back with both feet

2. Next, split your stance by taking a small step back with the leg on the same side as the handle. Your arm should be slightly in front of you

3. Fix your upper arm to your side

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

5. With your upper arm locked in position, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the handle up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed in position

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl up the handle, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Handle Reverse Cable Curl
description:
1. Attach a handle to the bottom of a cable machine and stand facing it. Carefully pick up the handle in one hand with an overhand grip and take a step back with both feet

2. Next, split your stance by taking a small step back with the leg on the same side as the handle. Your arm should be slightly in front of you

3. Fix your upper arm to your side

4. Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

5. With your upper arm locked in position, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the handle up in a controlled fashion

6. Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and forearms, keeping your upper arm fixed in position

7. Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl up the handle, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

8. Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep, forearm and core

9. Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

name: Single Kettlebell Clean and Reverse Lunge
Single Kettlebell Deficit Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Stand with both feet on a step or plate to elevate yourself (more elevation requires greater strength and mobility)

3. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

4. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg the same side as the kettlebell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

5. Keep on the toes for the back foot

6. The front leg should finish at an angle smaller than the front leg and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

7. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

8. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

9. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Kettlebell Lateral Step Up
description:
1. Stand parallel to a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in the hand that is furthest away from the box and have your arms by your side

3. Place the inside foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping down with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Single Kettlebell Lunge
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with the leg that is on the opposite side to the dumbbell. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes with the back foot

5. The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Kettlebell Rack Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Position the kettlebell in a rack position at the front of the shoulder. The kettlebell should be resting on the outside of the forearm, elbow pointing down, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Single Kettlebell Rack Squat
description:
1. Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand. Then clean it up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

3. Once the foot position is set and the kettlebells are in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

4. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

5. Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

6. Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

7. When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

8. Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

9. Pause and repeat

name: Single Kettlebell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and grab at the top of the horns with both hands. The kettlebell should be in front of your thighs

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the kettlebell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The kettlebell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the kettlebell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Single Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
description:
1. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand a kettlebell to you.

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg the same side as the kettlebell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Kettlebell Reverse Lunge/Overhead Press
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand. Then clean it up into a front rack position or have a partner assist you

2. Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

3. Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with the leg that is on the same side as the kettlebell. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

4. Keep on the toes for the back foot

5. The front leg should also be at a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

6. Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

7. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

8. From here, pause and drive the kettlebell straight up while at the same time externally rotating the arm. Your palm should finish facing forward at the top position

9. Ensure the shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during the pressing movement

10. Proceed to lower the kettlebell back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core, and glutes.

11. Return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Single Kettlebell Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you kettlebell in one hand and have arms by your side

3. Place the foot that’s opposite to the kettlebell flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

5. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

6. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

7. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Single Kettlebell Swing
description:
1. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and grab at the top of the horns with one hand. The kettlebell should be in front of your thighs with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lat (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by taking a big inhale and then hinging at the hips and pulling the kettlebell back between the legs, keeping the knees soft

5. The upper arm should be against the ribs and the forearm high in the groin. The free arm should be straight by your side

6. Once the hips are loaded and you have reached the end of your range of motion, reverse the motion by explosively driving your hips forward while pushing down into the ground with the feet

7. Exhale as you drive your hips forward and contract your glutes at the top

8. Bring the body to a standing position. Once the kettlebell is less than shoulder height, let it come back down on the same arc it went up

9. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row - 90 Degree Abduction
description:
1) Assume a kneeling position on the bench with your same side hand braced against the bench.

2) The foot on the floor should be in a stable position.

3) Brace your core and carefully pick up a dumbbell with the opposite hand with a pronated grip, palm facing your leg behind you.

4) Begin the movement by retracting your shoulder blade and bringing the dumbbell up by driving the elbow up and out to your side

5) Finish with your arm at a ninety-degree angle.

6) At the top of the movement, pause and squeeze your back muscles.

7) Proceed to then reverse the movement and lower the weight in a slow, controlled manner.

8) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout your upper back and core.

name: Single-Arm Overhead Dumbbell Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Lift the dumbbell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Single-Arm Overhead Dumbbell Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Lift the dumbbell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Single-Arm Overhead Kettlebell Carry
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Single-Arm Overhead Kettlebell Hold
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

2. Lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

3. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

4. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

5. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Single-Leg Barbell Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

3. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet along your centre line and elevate one leg up. You can keep your leg straight or bent, whatever you prefer

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

9. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

name: Single-Leg DB Glute Bridge w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a dumbbell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)
