5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

6. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Dumbbell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a dumbbell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

4. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

5. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Dumbbell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the dumbbell off the floor. At the same time lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner

6. Your torso, the dumbbell, and your back leg should all move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

7. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when the dumbbell is moving towards the floor

8. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

9. Lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the dumbbell up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

11. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Single-Leg Dumbbell RDL - Shin On Bench
description:
1. Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in one hand

2. Stood in front of a bench, elevate the foot on the same side as the dumbbell behind you so the front of your foot/shin is on the bench

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lowering the dumbbell in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep your back straight throughout

6. Your torso and the dumbbell should move at the same time

7. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

8. The foot on the bench is purely for balance so keep the majority of your weight on the front foot

9. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the dumbbell back up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Single-Leg Dumbbell RDL To Row
description:
**Should be proficient in both a single leg dumbbell RDL and a single dumbbell row before attempting

1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and grab a dumbbell in one hand

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the dumbbell off the floor. At the same time lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner

5. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when the dumbbell is moving towards the floor

6. Lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, row the dumbbell up towards your torso by driving your elbow to your side. Pause and then lower the dumbbell in a slow controlled manner, keeping tension in the lats, core and middle back

8. Then drive the hips forward and pull the dumbbell up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

9. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Single-Leg Elbow and Knee Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched

2. Bend the leg on the floor to a 90-degree angle so your foot is behind you. The top leg should be straight with your foot on the floor

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line from your knee to shoulder, keeping your knee on the floor

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Single-Leg Glute Bride w Mini Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

6. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your center line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

4. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

5. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Hip Thrust
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

3. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

4. Next, position your feet along your centre line and elevate one leg up. You can keep your leg straight or bent, whatever you prefer

5. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

6. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

7. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Single-Leg Hip Thrust w Band
description:
1. Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

4. With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

5. Next, position your feet along your centre line and elevate one leg up. You can keep your leg straight or bent, whatever you prefer

6. Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

7. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then drive your hips up by pushing through your heel. Think of using your glute to push your hips rather than your spine

8. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

9. Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

name: Single-Leg Kettlebell Glute Bridge
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

3. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

4. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

5. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Kettlebell Glute Bridge w Band
description:
1. Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

2. Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

3. Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

4. Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a dumbbell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

5. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

6. The higher you raise your top leg, the greater the resistance. The aim is to keep the same resistance in the band as you raise and lower your hips

7. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

name: Single-Leg Kettlebell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the dumbbell off the floor. At the same time lower the kettlebell in a controlled manner

6. Your torso, the kettlebell, and your back leg should all move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

7. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when the kettlebell is moving towards the floor

8. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

9. Lower the kettlebell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

10. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the kettlebell up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

11. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Single-Leg Kettlebell RDL to Row
description:
**Should be proficient in both a single leg dumbbell RDL and a single dumbbell row before attempting

1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and grab a dumbbell in one hand

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting the leg on the same side as the dumbbell off the floor. At the same time lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner

5. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when the dumbbell is moving towards the floor

6. Lower the dumbbell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, row the dumbbell up towards your torso by driving your elbow to your side. Pause and then lower the dumbbell in a slow controlled manner, keeping tension in the lats, core and middle back

8. Then drive the hips forward and pull the dumbbell up, while bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

9. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Single-Leg RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting one leg off the floor. The arm which is the same side as the moving leg should be straight and moving parallel to the standing leg

4. Your torso and back leg should move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

5. Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when your torso is moving towards the floor

6. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

7. Lower your torso in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

9. Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

name: Single-Leg RDL - Shin On Bench
description:
1. Stood in front of a bench, elevate one foot behind you so the front of your foot/shin is on the bench

2. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

3. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

4. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lowering your torso in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep your back straight throughout

5. The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

6. The foot on the bench is purely for balance so keep the majority of your weight on the front foot

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and bringing your torso back up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

name: Single-Leg Straight Arm and Knee Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

2. Bend the leg on the floor to a 90-degree angle so your foot is behind you. The top leg should be straight with your foot on the floor

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight, keeping your knee on the floor

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Snatch Grip Barbell RDL
description:
1. Start with your feet hip-width apart behind a barbell

2. Grab the barbell with a snatch grip and carefully deadlift the bar up or lift it up off a rack

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The barbell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Soleus Stretch
description:
1. Stand behind a chair or wall, anything that can support your weight

2. Take a small step back with one foot so the toes are aligned with the opposite heel

3. Start by carefully pushing the back knee forward while keeping both feet flat to the floor

4. Your should feel a stretch in the back of your lower leg

5. Hold for allotted time

name: Split Squat
description:
1. Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

2. The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground. Arms straight by your sides

3. To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

4. Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

5. From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Split Stance Cable Row
description:
1. Stand in front of a cable machine with a handle attached and have feet shoulder-width apart. Adjust the height so the handle is just below your chest when stood

2. Grab a handle in one hand with your palm facing in and take a step back with the leg on the opposite side. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blade, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Split Stance Cable Single Chest Press
description:
1. Attach a handle to a cable machine so it’s at chest height

2. Grab the handle with one hand and take a step away so your back is to the machine. Split your stance by stepping back with the leg that’s the same side as the handle

3. Begin by getting into an upright posture with a neutral spine, with your shoulders depressed down and shoulder blades squeezed

4. Starting with your arm straight in front and palm facing down, slowly bring the handle back in a controlled manner by bending the elbow and bringing the handle just to the outside of your chest

5. Your arm should finish in a 45-degree to the body

6. Be careful not to rotate your torso during the movement and ensure shoulder remains down

7. Proceed to reverse the movement by pressing the handle forward while bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top

8. Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core

name: Split Stance Dumbbell Row
description:
1) Begin by standing behind an incline bench (between 30 – 45-degrees) and taking a step back with one foot.

2) With your same side hand braced against the bench. The foot on the floor should be in a stable position.

3) Brace your core and carefully pick up a kettlebell with the opposite hand with a neutral grip.

4) Begin the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and bringing the kettlebell up by driving the elbow back.

5) Pull the kettlebell up towards the body, in a slight arcing motion, until your elbow is level (or just past) with your torso. The kettlebell should finish towards your hip with your arm in a ninety-degree angle.

6) At the top of the movement, pause and squeeze your back muscles.

7) Proceed to then reverse the movement and lower the weight in a slow, controlled manner.

8) Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, upper back and core.

name: Split Stance Pallof Press
description:
1) Stand parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height, with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointed forward

2) Take a small step back with the leg furthest away from the machine or band so you’re in a split stance

3) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

4) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

5) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

6) Hands should go out in a straight line

7) Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

8) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

name: Split Stance Pallof Press Hold
description:
1) Stand parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height, with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointed forward

2) Take a small step back with the leg furthest away from the machine or band so you’re in a split stance

3) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

4) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

5) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

6) Pause in this position and hold for the allotted time

7) Your core should be engaged with as minimal movement in the body as possible for the entire duration of the set

8) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

9) When time finishes, return the handle or band in a slow controlled manner

name: Split Stance Single Arm Trap 3 Raise
Sprinter Calf Stretch
description:
1. Get onto your hands and knees on the floor

2. Then straighten your legs and place your feet beside each other

3. Start by pushing one heel to the floor while bringing the other one up

4. You should feel a stretch in the back of your lower leg

5. Alternate legs for allotted time

name: Standard Dip
description:
1. Stand on a step with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart on a dip bar

2. Your legs should be straight with your feet slightly behind you on the step. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft

3. Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine and that your shoulders are depressed down

4. Start by lifting your feet from the step and holding your weight

5. Then begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, maintaining constant tension in your triceps

6. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

7. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

8. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

name: Standard Dip - Leg Assist
description:
1. Stand on a step with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart on a dip bar

2. Your legs should be straight with your feet slightly behind you on the step. Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft

3. Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine and that your shoulders are depressed down

4. Start the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, using your legs to assist you as you move down. Maintain constant tension in your triceps

5. Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

6. Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

7. Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, using your legs to assist you as much as needed. Contract your triceps when you reach the top

name: Standing Alternating Dumbbell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in each hand

2. Position them at the top of your shoulders, with palms facing inwards towards the midline of the body and your elbows tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive one of dumbbells above the head

4. Make sure the dumbbell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

7. Repeat on the other side

name: Standing Alternating Kettlebell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a kettlebell in each hand hand

2. Position the kettlebells in a rack position at the front of the shoulders. Kettlebells should be resting on the outside of the forearms, elbows pointing down, with the palms facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. Make sure to engage your core and glutes

4. From this position, drive one of the kettlebells straight up while at the same time externally rotating the arm. Your palm should finish facing forward at the top position

5. Make sure the kettlebell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

6. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

7. Proceed to lower the kettlebell back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

8. Repeat on the other side

name: Standing Band Shoulder Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and place a band under each foot

2. Grab the band with each hand and position them at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing forwards. Elbows tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the band above the head

4. Make sure the band finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the biceps in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the band back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Standing Banded Clam
description:
1. Place a band just above your knees and place your feet hip-width apart, just enough to create some tension in the band

2. With a neutral spine, hinge at the hips slightly by pushing your bum back, your knees should be in line with your toes and slightly bent

3. Interlock your hands out in front to help with balance

4. Start by rotating one of your legs by moving it at a 45-degree angle, bringing it from the floor, and externally rotating your hip in the process

5. The standing leg should stationary, making sure to keep the knee aligned over the foot and resist any inward movement. This leg provides resistance by stretching the band

6. Pause at the end and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, touching your foot on the floor if needed

7. The rest of your body should stay stationary, with the movement coming solely from the hip rotating

name: Standing Cable Cuff Lateral Raise
description:
1. Stand facing a cable machine and have your feet shoulder-width apart. Attach two cuffs to the bottom

2. Put your hand in the opposite cuff so the cables cross each other in front of the body. Your hands should be by your side with your palms facing your body. Ensure you don’t rest on your sides

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and raising your arms to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your elbows at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the side of your shoulders and core

6. Hands shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Standing Cable Lateral Raise
description:
1. Stand facing a cable machine and have your feet shoulder-width apart. Attach two handles to the bottom

2. Grab the handles with the opposite hand so the cables cross each other in front of the body. Your hands should be by your side with your palms facing your body. Ensure you don’t rest on your sides

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and raising your arms to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your elbows at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the side of your shoulders and core

6. Handles shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Standing Cable Single Lateral Raise
description:
1) Stand sideways on to cable machine. with the pulley at the bottom of the machine.

2) Grab the handle so the cable goes across and in front of your thighs.

3) Start by raising your arm up and out to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbow should remain soft throughout and your wrist shouldn’t go above your elbow at any point.

4) Pause and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension on the side of your shoulder.

name: Standing Calf Raise
description:
1. Stand on the edge of an elevated surface such as a step or weight plate. Step on with both feet at hip-width apart

2. Hold onto something to keep you balanced such as a wall or squat rack

3. Ensure you have a neutral spine and that your legs are straight

4. Begin by dropping your heels as low as they can go in a slow controlled manner towards the floor while keeping the front of your feet on the step

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then drive through your toes and raise your heels as high as you can

6. Squeeze your calf’s in this position and then repeat

name: Standing Dual Pulley Cable Row
description:
1. Stand in front of a cable machine with two handles attached and have feet shoulder-width apart. Adjust the height so the handles are just below your chest when stood

2. Grab a handle in each hand with your palms facing each other and take a step back. Brace yourself with your feet

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side so your hands finish beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

5. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

6. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
description:
1. With a dumbbell in one hand, stand on the edge of an elevated surface such as a step or weight plate. Step on with both feet at hip-width apart

2. Place the dumbbell straight by your side and hold onto something to keep you balanced such as a wall or squat rack with the other hand

3. Ensure you have a neutral spine and that your legs are straight

4. Begin by dropping your heels as low as they can go in a slow controlled manner towards the floor while keeping the front of your feet on the step

5. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then drive through your toes and raise your heels as high as you can

6. Squeeze your calf’s in this position and then repeat

name: Standing Dumbbell Lateral Raise
description:
1. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart with a dumbbell in each hand

2. Dumbbells should be by your side with your palms facing your body. Ensure the dumbbells don’t rest on your sides

3. Your head should be looking forward with a neutral spine, with your shoulder blades pinched and shoulders down

4. Begin by bracing your core and raising your arms to your side, stopping at shoulder height. Your elbows should remain soft throughout and your wrists shouldn’t go above your elbows at any point

5. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in the side of your shoulders and core

6. Dumbbells shouldn’t touch your body when lowering

name: Standing Dumbbell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in each hand

2. Position them at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body and with the elbows tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbells above the head

4. Make sure the dumbbells finish shoulder width and above the head, with the biceps in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the dumbbells back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Standing Dumbbell Push Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in each hand

2. Position them at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body and with the elbows tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, dip slightly at the knees and hips, then press the dumbbells to lockout overhead by extending your legs and arms simultaneously

4. Make sure the dumbbells finish above the head at shoulder width, with the biceps in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the dumbbells back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Standing Dumbbell Single Calf Raise
description:
1. With a dumbbell in one hand, stand on the edge of an elevated surface such as a step or weight plate. Step on with both feet at hip-width apart

2. Place the dumbbell straight by your side and hold onto something to keep you balanced such as a wall or squat rack with the other hand

3. Bring one foot behind you so it’s no longer on the step. You can hook it around the standing leg or keep it elevated just behind you

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine

5. Begin by dropping your heel as low as it can go in a slow controlled manner towards the floor while keeping the front of your foot on the step

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then drive through your toes and raise your heel as high as you can

7. Squeeze your calf in this position and then repeat

name: Standing Dumbbell Single-Arm Push Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in one hand

2. Position it at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body and your elbow tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, dip slightly at the knees and hips, then press the dumbbell to lockout overhead by extending your legs and arms simultaneously

4. Make sure the dumbbell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

name: Standing Kettlebell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a kettlebell in each hand

2. Position the kettlebells in a rack position at the front of the shoulders. Kettlebells should be resting on the outside of the forearms, elbows pointing down, with the palms facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. Make sure to engage your core and glutes

4. From this position, drive the kettlebells straight up while at the same time externally rotating the arms. Your palms should finish facing forward at the top position

5. Make sure the kettlebells finish above the head at shoulder width, with the biceps in line with the ears

6. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

7. Proceed to lower the kettlebells back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Standing Kettlebell Push Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a kettlebell in each hand

2. Position the kettlebells in a rack position at the front of the shoulders. Kettlebells should be resting on the outside of the forearms, elbows pointing down, with the palms facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, dip slightly at the knees and hips and press the kettlebells to lockout overhead by extending your legs and arms simultaneously. Externally rotate the arms as you press the kettlebells so your palms finish facing forwards at the top position

4. Make sure the kettlebells finish above the head at shoulder width, with the biceps in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the kettlebells back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

name: Standing Pallof Press
description:
1) Stand parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height, with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointed forward

2) Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3) Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4) From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5) Hands should go out in a straight line

6) Pause and then proceed to return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the core at all times

7) Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

name: Standing Pallof Press Hold
description:
1. Stand parallel to a cable machine or band at chest height, with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointed forward

2. Grab the handle or band with both hands and position in front of your chest

3. Spine should be in a neutral position and a tall upright posture should be attained

4. From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, push your hands out in front till the arms straighten

5. Pause in this position and hold for the allotted time

6. Your core should be engaged with as minimal movement in the body as possible for the entire duration of the set

7. Make sure to keep shoulders depressed down at all times throughout the movement

8. When time finishes, return the handle or band in a slow controlled manner

name: Standing Single Calf Raise
description:
1. Stand on the edge of an elevated surface such as a step or weight plate. Step on with both feet at hip-width apart

2. Hold onto something to keep you balanced such as a wall or squat rack

3. Bring one foot behind you so it’s no longer on the step. You can hook it around the standing leg or keep it elevated just behind you

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine

5. Begin by dropping your heel as low as it can go in a slow controlled manner towards the floor while keeping the front of your foot on the step

6. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then drive through your toes and raise your heel as high as you can

7. Squeeze your calf in this position and then repeat

name: Standing Single-Arm Dumbbell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in one hand

2. Position it at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body and your elbow tucked in and pointed down

3. From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbell above the head

4. Make sure the dumbbell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

5. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

6. Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

name: Standing Single-Arm Kettlebell Press
description:
1. Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a kettlebell in one hand

2. Position the kettlebell in a rack position at the front of the shoulder. Kettlebell should be resting on the outside of the forearm, elbow pointing down, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body

3. Make sure to engage your core and glutes

4. From this position, drive the kettlebell straight up while at the same time externally rotating the arm. Your palm should finish facing forward at the top position

5. Make sure the kettlebell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

6. Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

7. Proceed to lower the kettlebell back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

name: Step Down
description:
1. Stand with both feet on a step, shoulder-width apart and facing forward

2. Begin by engaging your core, then bend one leg in a slow controlled manner while bringing the other foot forward so the heel touches the floor

3. Keep the knee of the standing leg tracking the middle of your foot as you move

4. Keep the foot close to the step as you bring it towards the floor

5. The front leg should be kept stationary and the foot flat throughout the movement

6. Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

7. From here, return to the start position by driving off the standing foot and maintaining an upright torso

name: Step Up
description:
1. Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

2. Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine. Hands can be by your side or out in front for balance

3. Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

4. There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

5. Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

6. From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

name: Sternocleidomastoid Stretch
description:
1. Can do stood or seated

2. Ensure you have a neutral spine and you have a nice upright posture

3. Start by turning your head to the side and tilting up, then place your hand on the opposite side of your head

4. Position your other arm behind your back

5. Then carefully pull your head to the side while keeping your head facing upwards

6. You should feel a stretch in the side of your neck

7. Be careful not to pull down too hard and make sure you maintain posture throughout

8. Hold for allotted time

name: Straight Arm and Knee Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

2. Bend your knees so they’re in a 90-degree angle with your feet behind you

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight, keeping your knee on the floor

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Straight Arm Plank
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

3. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

4. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

5. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Straight Arm Plank - Band Around Hips
Straight Arm Plank - Shoulders 120 Degrees
description:
1. Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

2. Next, position your hands further forward than a regular plank so there's a 120-degree angle in your shoulders

2. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core. Then extend your legs back one at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

3. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

4. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

5. Maintain this position for the allotted time, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Straight Arm Side Plank
description:
1. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Place your other hand on your top hip or flat by your side

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Straight Arm Side Plank Cable Row
description:
1. Position a handle about two foot from the floor on a cable machine. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Grab the handle with your hand and straighten your arm out in front

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

6. Next, retract your shoulder blade and then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Straight Arm Side Plank with Band Hold
description:
1. Anchor a band and hold in one hand with your arm straight

2. Then lie on the side that has no band with your hand flat against the floor. There should be no slack in the band

3. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

4. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes. The aim is to stop yourself rotating forward with the band

name: Straight Arm Side Plank with Dumbbell Overhead
description:
1. Lie on your side with one hand flat against the floor

2. Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

3. You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

4. Grab a dumbbell with your free hand

5. Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight. Then pull the dumbbell up so your arm is straight and perpendicular to the floor. Your shoulder, elbow and wrist should be in a straight line

6. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

name: Straight Bar Pressdown
description:
1. Facing a cable machine, set up a straight bar at the top. With an overhand grip, grab with both hands

2. The cable should be directly in front of you

3. Start with your arms bent and fixed into your side. Your upper arms should be perpendicular with the floor and should stay so for the entire movement

4. Keep your shoulders pinned down and engage your core

5. Begin the movement by pressing the bar down and extending your arms until straight, but not fully locked out

6. Keep your elbows fixed and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

7. Pause at the bottom and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

name: Supinated Bent Over Row
description:
1. Stand behind a barbell with your feet shoulder-width apart

2. Carefully lift the barbell up either by deadlifting it or lifting it up from a rack with an underhand grip, just wider than shoulder-width apart

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Supine Banded Knee to Chest (Psoas Focused)
Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch
description:
1. Lie on the floor with your legs straight and your arms flat by your side

2. You should have a neutral spine with your head on the floor. You can place a support under your head if desired

3. Start by carefully lifting one leg off the floor and placing the opposite hand behind the far side of the knee

4. Then slowly pull the leg across your body while also pulling it towards you

5. Your should feel a stretch in the outer back-side of your leg

6. Ensure your back doesn't come off the floor

7. Hold for allotted time

name: Supine From Hands Banded Hip Abduction
Swiss Ball Body Saw
description:
1. Set up on your knees with a swiss ball in front of you, ensuring you have a neutral spine

2. Then place your forearms on the ball and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the ball and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Begin by pushing the ball forward with your forearms in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep your hips up and your core engaged

7. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then roll the ball back to the start position

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Swiss Ball Leg Curl
description:
1. Set up on your back with your feet elevated on a swiss ball and your arms flat by your side

2. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis by tucking your tail bone in and then brace your core. You should have a neutral spine for the entire movement

3. Start by extending your hips so they raise off the floor. You should have a straight line from your head to your heels.

4. Begin the movement by bringing the ball in towards your glutes by pulling your heels in towards you, making sure to keep the hips extended at all times

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, glutes and hamstrings while maintaining a neutral spine

6. If you find yourself getting a pump through your lower back then you’re likely substituting spinal extension for hip extension, which is a fault in technique

name: Swiss Ball Leg Raise
description:
1. Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs straight and your arms straight by your side

2. Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

3. Place both feet either side of a swiss and squeeze

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise both legs and lift the ball from the floor. As you raise your legs it’s important that you keep your back flat to the floor

5. When you reach the top, pause and then lower the ball to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure you keep your legs straight. Keep your belly button pushed down and exhale as you lower, keeping constant tension in your core

6. Gently touch the floor with the ball and repeat

name: Swiss Ball Plank
description:
1. Set up on your knees with a swiss ball in front of you, ensuring you have a neutral spine

2. Then place your forearms on the ball and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then simultaneously push the ball forward with your forearms and extend your hips in a slow controlled manner

5. As you move forward your torso will lower towards the floor but ensure your hips stay level. Your hips shouldn’t drop towards the floor and be careful not to extend your spine

6. Your arms will begin to straighten as you roll forward

7. Keep your head neutral and looking at the ball, making sure to keep a neutral spine and constant tension in your core

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause slightly and then return to the start position by pulling the ball back towards you. Your torso should raise up as you pull in towards yourself

9. Be careful to never pull the roller underneath yourself

name: Swiss Ball Rollouts
description:
1. Set up on your knees with a swiss ball in front of you, ensuring you have a neutral spine

2. Then place your forearms on the ball and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Begin the move by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then simultaneously push the ball forward with your forearms and extend your hips in a slow controlled manner

5. As you move forward your torso will lower towards the floor but ensure your hips stay level. Your hips shouldn’t drop towards the floor and be careful not to extend your spine

6. Your arms will begin to straighten as you roll forward

7. Keep your head neutral and looking at the ball, making sure to keep a neutral spine and constant tension in your core

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause slightly and then return to the start position by pulling the ball back towards you. Your torso should raise up as you pull in towards yourself

9. Be careful to never pull the roller underneath yourself

name: Swiss Ball Single-Leg Curl
description:
1. Set up on your back with your one foot elevated on a swiss ball and your arms flat by your side. Other foot in the air

2. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis by tucking your tail bone in and then brace your core. You should have a neutral spine for the entire movement

3. Start by extending your hips so they raise off the floor. You should have a straight line from your head to your heels.

4. Begin the movement by bringing the ball in towards your glutes by pulling your heel in towards you, making sure to keep the hips extended at all times

5. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, glutes and hamstrings while maintaining a neutral spine

6. If you find yourself getting a pump through your lower back then you’re likely substituting spinal extension for hip extension, which is a fault in technique

name: Swiss Ball Stir The Pot
description:
1. Set up on your knees with a swiss ball in front of you, ensuring you have a neutral spine

2. Then place your forearms on the ball and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

3. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend one leg back at a time in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the ball and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Begin the movement by moving your forearms in a clockwise direction in a controlled manner. Pause when you return to the start position and repeat going in the opposite direction

7. The larger the circle the greater the difficulty, so find what you’re capable of performing with the correct form

8. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: Terminal Knee Extension
description:
1. Anchor a band at knee height and place it around the back one of your knees, facing the band

2. Stand nice and tall with a neutral spine and your arms by your side

3. Feet hip-width apart and facing forward

4. Start by bending your knee forward in a controlled manner and slightly raising your heel from the floor

5. The movement should be solely from the knee. The rest of the body should stay stationary, particularly your spine and hips

6. When you can’t go any further with your knee, drive your heel into the floor and extend your leg by driving the knee back against the band

7. Squeeze your quad (front of thigh) and glute when you bring your knee back

name: Thumb and Hand Sequence - Lacrosse Ball
Thumb and Hand Smash and Floss
Toe Touch Progression
description:
1. Start with your feet together and the balls of your feet on a foam pad, block, etc

2. Stand tall with an upright posture and engage your core. Your knees should be soft and not locked out

3. Position a rolled-up towel or yoga block in between your knees, holding it tightly in position

4. Start by reaching your hands towards the ceiling and fully extending your arms, then take a deep breath in and on the exhale bring your chin toward your chest and bend forward so that your fingertips reach to touch your toes

5. If you don't reach your toes with your fingertips, then bend your knees until you reach your toes to complete the movement on each repetition

6. Return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, repeat for desired reps

7. Then walk forward and change your foot position so that your heels are elevated and your toes are on the ground in front of the block

8. From here repeat the same movement sequence for desired reps

name: Toes Elevated Kettlebell RDL
description:
1. Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and grab at the top of the horns with both hands. The kettlebell should be in front of your thighs

2. Start with your feet hip-width apart and step onto a plate or a wedge to raise your toes

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the kettlebell in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. The kettlebell should stay close to the body and should travel up and down in a straight line over the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the kettlebell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Towel Squeezes
description:
1. Grab a hand towel and squeeze it as hard as possible with your hand.

2. Your hand should be half-open as you're squeezing.

3. Keep squeezing for the allotted period of time.

4. Relax and then repeat.

name: Trap Bar Bent Over Row
description:
1. Stand behind a trap bar with your feet shoulder-width apart

2. Carefully lift the barbell up either by deadlifting it or lifting it up from a rack

3. A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

4. Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

5. Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the barbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

6. Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

7. Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

name: Trap Bar Carry
description:
1. Carefully lift up a trap bar

2. Feet should be normal walking stance width, with your head facing forward, and make sure you have a neutral spine

3. Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

4. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

name: Trap Bar Deadlift and Carry
description:
1. Start by getting into your deadlift set up in a trap bar and lifting the bar up

2. Pause slightly when stood, making sure your feet are normal walking stance width and your head is facing forward

3. Then brace your core and maintain a nice upright posture, ensuring you don’t lose your neutral spine

4. Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

5. Pause and then carefully lower the bar to the floor with the lowering phase of the deadlift

6. When the bar is on the floor let go of the bar and then turn around to repeat

name: Trap Bar Deadlift From Blocks
description:
1. Elevate the bar with blocks, bumper plates, etc

2. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

3. Stand in the middle of the trap bar, with the middle of your foot in line with the center of the handles (this helps with balance)

4. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side
5. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

6. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

7. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

8. Shove your knees out a little, then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

9. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, lifting the bar off the floor. Keep the bar path in as straight a vertical line as possible)

10. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

11. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar travel down in a straight vertical line

12. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

13. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Trap Bar Deadlift.
description:
1. Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

2. Stand in the middle of the trap bar, with the middle of your foot in line with the center of the handles (this helps with balance)

3. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side

4. Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

5. Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

6. It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

7. Shove your knees out a little,then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

8. Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, lifting the bar off the floor. Keep the bar path in as straight a vertical line as possible)

9. Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

10. Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar travel down in a straight vertical line

11. As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

12. Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

name: Trap Bar RDL
description:
1. Step into a trap bar and have your feet hip-width apart

2. Grab the bar and carefully deadlift the bar up

3. Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

4. From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

5. Begin the movement by hinging at the hips. While maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the knees soft, push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum) and lower the bar in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

6. You want the bar to travel up and down in a straight line with your weight being in the middle of the foot

7. Make sure to keep the knees soft and avoid locking them out

8. When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the barbell up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

name: Trunk Stability Rotation Knees Flexed
description:
1. On the floor, lay on your back and position your knees and hips at 90-degree angles. Place a towel or foam roller between your knees

2. Place your arms straight by your side with one hand palm down and the other with the palm facing up

3. Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then rotate your knees in a slow controlled manner to the side with the palm facing down, keeping the opposite shoulder blade down in the process

4. When you reach the bottom position, pause slightly and then return to the start position

5. Then rotate to the other side, rotating your hands in the process

6. Return to the start position, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

name: TRX Body Saw
description:
1. Adjust a TRX to mid-shin height

2. With your back to the TRX, set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Put both into the TRX straps and make sure they’re level

4. Then go onto your forearms and grasp your hands together, leaving a 45-degree angle between your forearms

5. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend both legs back in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

6. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

7. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

8. Begin the move by pushing through your forearms and moving your body backward in a slow controlled manner

9. Go back to a position where you can keep your form. Pause at this point and return to the start position. Keep your core engaged throughout

10. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: TRX Chest Press
description:
1. Stand facing a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Bring your hands out in front so there is no slack on the straps

2. Position your hands just wider than shoulder-width and in line with the middle of your chest, with your hands facing down. Pinch your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders down

3. Feet flat or up on toes. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your eyes looking forward. A straight line should run from the back of your foot to your head

4. The smaller the angle between your body and the floor, the greater the difficulty. Find a position that suits your ability.

5. Begin by bracing your core and bending your elbows, lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner. Arms should be at a 45-degree angle to your body. Make sure you keep a neutral spine throughout the movement

6. Lower yourself so your hands are to the outside of your chest. Pause slightly and then reverse the movement by pressing through the handles with your hands and extending your elbows

7. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement

8. Make sure you keep constant tension in your chest and core

name: TRX Fly
description:
1. Stand facing a TRX and grab a handle with each hand. Bring your hands out in front so there is no slack on the straps

2. Position your hands just wider than shoulder-width and in line with the middle of your chest, palms facing in. Pinch your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders down

3. Feet flat or up on toes. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your eyes looking forward. A straight line should run from the back of your foot to your head

4. The smaller the angle between your body and the floor, the greater the difficulty. Find a position that suits your ability.

5. With your core braced and elbows slightly unlocked, begin by lowering yourself by bringing your arms to the outside of your body. Make sure you keep a neutral spine throughout the movement

6. Lower yourself so your hands are just past your chest. Pause slightly and then reverse the movement by pressing through the handles with your hands and bringing them towards each other. Hands should return to starting position

7. Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement

8. Make sure you keep constant tension in your chest and core

name: TRX Knees To Chest
description:
1. Adjust a TRX to mid-shin height

2. With your back to the TRX, set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Ensure you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

3. Put both into the TRX straps and make sure they’re level

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend both legs back in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Create tension in your hands, shoulders, and upper back to help maintain position

7. Begin the move by bringing both knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep a neutral spine in the process

8. Pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner

9. Your shoulder blades shouldn’t pinch together at any point. Make sure to keep your spine in a neutral position and your hips parallel with the floor

name: TRX Pike
description:
1. Adjust a TRX to mid-shin height

2. With your back to the TRX, set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Ensure you have a neutral spine

3. Put both into the TRX straps and make sure they’re level

4. Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core. Then extend both legs back in a controlled manner, making sure to keep your core engaged

5. Your head should be in a neutral position looking at the floor and feet roughly hip-width apart

6. Create tension in your hands, forearms, and upper back to help maintain position

7. Begin the move by pushing your hips towards the ceiling and pulling your feet in while keeping your legs straight

8. Make sure you keep a neutral spine for the entire movement. When you reach the top, pause and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner
